5. **Start off only when it is safe.** Start the engine, look carefully in all directions for boats, swimmers, and obstacles, then accelerate smoothly to planing speed.

<PIC>

**Important safety reminders:**
- Do **not** apply throttle until the passenger is fully seated, has both feet in the footwell, and is holding on securely.
- Before starting off, always make sure there are no boats, obstructions, or swimmers nearby.
- The watercraft is designed for the operator and **one passenger only**. Do not exceed a total load of **160 kg / 353 lb**, including operator, passenger, and any cargo.
- If the watercraft feels difficult to balance at a standstill, take extra time to stabilize it before accelerating.,['Manual20_90', 'Manual20_91', 'Manual20_92']

If the jetski is difficult to balance while stopped, use the following boarding method. This is especially important when riding with a passenger, because the heavier the total load, the harder it is to keep the watercraft stable at a standstill.

1. Have the passenger steady the jetski first. The passenger should remain in the water and hold the watercraft steady while the operator boards from the rear. The operator should pull himself onto the boarding platform, move into a seated position, and then balance the watercraft.

<PIC>

2. Once the operator is seated and balanced, attach the engine shut-off cord/lanyard to your left wrist, then install the clip on the engine shut-off switch. Start the engine and keep it at idle speed.

3. With the operator seated and controlling the jetski, the passenger should pull himself onto the rear platform, kneel down, and then carefully crawl forward onto the seat as the watercraft begins to accelerate.

<PIC>

4. After both riders are properly seated, the operator may accelerate the jetski to planing speed. Once the watercraft is stable on plane, reduce the throttle to the desired running speed.

<PIC>

Important safety reminders:
- Do not apply throttle until the passenger is seated, has both feet on the floor of the footwell, and is securely holding on to the operator.
- Before starting off, always check that there are no boats, swimmers, obstacles, or other hazards around you.
- Do not operate the watercraft if the combined weight of the operator, passenger, and cargo exceeds 160 kg / 353 lb.,['jetski_06', 'jetski_07', 'jetski_08']

The maximum load capacity of this jetski is 160 kg (353 lb). This maximum load includes the combined total weight of: - the operator, - one passenger, - and any cargo carried on board. The jetski is designed to carry up to 2 people total: the operator and one passenger. For safety and proper handling, do not exceed the 160 kg / 353 lb load limit, and do not allow more than two people to ride at the same time.,['Manual20_12']

Here is an overview of the base station for the XL490 and XL495 landline models:

**For XL490**

The XL490 base station has a simple layout with one main button:

• **Find handset / Paging button**: Press this button to make the handset ring, which helps you locate it if it is misplaced nearby.  
• **Registration mode**: Press and hold this button to put the base station into registration mode when you need to register an additional handset.

<PIC>

**For XL495**

The XL495 base station includes the same handset locating and registration function, plus additional answering machine controls.

• **Find handset / Paging button**: Press to find registered handsets.  
• **Registration mode**: Press and hold to enter registration mode for pairing additional handsets.

<PIC>

**Additional XL495 base station controls**

The XL495 base station also includes these answering machine-related parts and buttons:

• **Speaker**: Plays answering machine messages and audio from the base station.  
• **Answering machine on/off button**: Turns the answering machine on or off.  
• **Delete button**: Deletes the message currently being played. Press and hold it to delete all old messages.  
• **Volume down / volume up buttons**: Decrease or increase the speaker volume.  
• **Skip backward / skip forward buttons**: Move to the previous or next message during playback.  
• **Play / stop button**: Starts playing messages or stops message playback.

In short, the XL490 base station mainly provides handset paging and registration functions, while the XL495 base station adds built-in answering machine controls for message playback, deletion, volume adjustment, and message navigation.,['Manual22_18', 'Manual22_19']

To connect the base station of your landline phone, please follow these steps:

1. Connect the power adapter

Plug one end of the supplied power adapter into the DC input jack located at the bottom of the base station. Then plug the other end into a wall power socket.

<PIC>

2. Connect the telephone line cord

Plug one end of the telephone line cord into the telephone socket at the bottom of the base station. Then plug the other end into the telephone wall socket.

<PIC>

Important safety reminders:

• Make sure the power supply voltage matches the voltage printed on the back or underside of the phone.
• Use only the supplied power adapter with the base station.

Additional notes:

• If your telephone line is also used for DSL high-speed internet, install a DSL filter between the telephone line cord and the wall telephone socket. This helps prevent noise and caller ID issues caused by DSL interference.
• In some countries or regions, you may need to connect a line adapter to the line cord first, and then plug it into the telephone wall socket.

After the base station is connected, place the handset on the base station to charge it before first use.,['Manual22_22', 'Manual22_23']

To install the handset, please follow these steps:

1. The rechargeable batteries are already pre-installed inside the handset.
2. Before charging the handset for the first time, pull out the battery tape from the battery door area. This allows the batteries to make proper contact and power the handset.

<PIC>

3. After removing the battery tape, place the handset on the base station to charge. Make sure it sits correctly in the cradle; you may hear a docking sound when it is properly positioned.
4. Charge the handset for 8 hours before first use.

Important: If you ever need to remove or replace the batteries, check the battery polarity carefully when inserting them into the battery compartment. Incorrect polarity may damage the product.

It is normal for the handset to feel slightly warm while charging.,['Manual22_23']

To check the battery level of your landline handset, simply look at the battery icon on the handset display. This icon shows the current battery status.

<PIC>

You can read the battery level as follows:

- Three bars: battery is full.
- Two bars: battery is at a medium level.
- One bar: battery is low.
- Empty battery icon flashing: the battery is very low and the handset needs to be recharged.

<PIC>

In standby mode, the main screen icons also help you confirm the battery status:

- When the handset is off the base station, the battery bars show the remaining battery level.
- When the handset is placed on the base station, the battery bars scroll/animate to show that charging is in progress.
- If the low-battery icon flashes and/or an alert tone sounds, place the handset back on the base station to recharge it.

<PIC>

Please note: if the batteries become completely empty, the handset will turn off. If you are on a call when the batteries are almost empty, you may hear warning tones, and the call may disconnect after the warning. To avoid this, recharge the handset as soon as the low-battery warning appears.,['Manual22_28', 'Manual22_29', 'Manual22_30']

If your handset shows “Searching”, it means the handset is not currently connected to the base station. Please try the following steps:

1. Check that the base station is powered on  
Make sure the power adapter is firmly connected to the base station and plugged into a working wall socket. If the base station has no power, the handset will keep searching and cannot connect.

<PIC>

2. Register the handset to the base station  
If the handset is not paired with the base station, it needs to be registered again. Press and hold the paging/registration button on the base station until the base enters registration mode, then complete the registration from the handset if prompted.

<PIC>

3. Move the handset closer to the base station  
The handset may be out of range. Place it near the base station and wait a moment to see if the signal returns. Once connected, the signal bars should appear on the handset screen.

If it still stays in “Searching” after these checks, try disconnecting the base station power for a short time, reconnect it, and then repeat the registration step.,['Manual22_22', 'Manual22_18']

You can check the current phone status by looking at the LED indicator behavior on both the handset and the base station.

For the handset, the LED indicator can show either event status or charging status, depending on the setting you choose. To set it, go to:

[Menu] > [Phone setup] > [LED status] > [Events status] / [Charge status], then press [OK] to confirm. The setting will be saved.

<PIC>

Handset LED meanings:

• Events status
  - On: Audio boost is on.
  - Off: The phone is in a call or in standby mode.
  - Flashing: There is a new message, new voicemail, missed call, or the phone is muted.

• Charging status
  - On: The handset is on the base or charging cradle in standby mode, or audio boost is on during a call.
  - Off: The handset is not on the base or charging cradle.
  - Flashing: The phone is muted.

For the base station, the LED indicator mainly shows answering machine, message, call, paging, and registration status.

<PIC>

Base station LED meanings:

• Steady on
  - There is no new message and the memory is not full.
  - The handset is switched on.
  - The phone has entered paging mode.

• Flashing
  - There is a new message and the memory is not full.
  - There is an incoming call.
  - The phone is recording an incoming message or announcement.
  - A message is being played back.
  - Remote access is in use, or messages are being listened to from the handset.

• Flashing fast
  - There is no new message, but the memory is full.
  - The phone has entered registration mode.

So, if you want to identify the current status quickly, first check whether the LED is on, off, flashing, or flashing fast, then match it with the handset or base station status shown above.,['Manual22_40', 'Manual22_46']

You can identify the current status of the landline base station by observing how the LED indicator behaves. Please refer to the LED status table below:

<PIC>

Here is what each LED behavior means:

**1. LED steady on**
This usually indicates a normal or active base-station status:
- There is **no new message**, and the answering machine memory is **not full**.
- The handset is **switched on**.
- The base station has entered **paging mode** when you are trying to find the handset.

**2. LED flashing**
A flashing LED means the base station is currently handling a call, message, or answering-machine activity:
- There is a **new message**, and the memory is **not full**.
- There is an **incoming call**.
- The base station is **recording an incoming message or announcement**.
- A message is being **played back**.
- Remote access is in progress, or messages are being listened to from the handset.

**3. LED flashing fast**
A fast-flashing LED usually indicates a condition that needs attention or a setup mode:
- There is **no new message**, but the answering machine memory is **full**.
- The base station has entered **registration mode**, for example when registering an additional handset.

So, in short: **steady on = normal/active status**, **flashing = new message/call or answering-machine activity**, and **fast flashing = memory full or registration mode**.,['Manual22_46']

To lower the roll bar, follow these steps carefully: Warning: When the roll bar is in the down position, the mower does not provide rollover protection. Lower it only when absolutely necessary, such as when you need temporary overhead clearance. Do not wear the seat belt while the roll bar is down. Drive slowly and carefully, check for overhead obstacles such as branches, doorways, or wires, and raise the roll bar again as soon as clearance permits. 1. Park the mower safely, engage the parking brake, shut off the engine, remove the key, and wait for all moving parts to stop. 2. Remove the hairpin cotters, then remove the 2 retaining pins that secure the roll bar. 3. Lower the roll bar into one of the available down positions. There are 2 down positions, as shown below. 4. Reinstall the 2 pins through the roll bar brackets to lock the roll bar in the selected down position. 5. Secure both pins with the hairpin cotters so the roll bar cannot move while operating. Once you have passed the low-clearance area, raise the roll bar back to the fully upright, locked position and use the seat belt when operating with the roll bar up.,['Manual23_32', 'Manual23_33']

For lawn mowers equipped with the suspension system, you can adjust the 2 rear-shock assemblies to make the ride softer or firmer according to your comfort preference.

<PIC>

The rear-shock slots include detent positions as reference points. These positions help you match both sides evenly, but the shock assemblies do not have to be placed only in the detents—you may position them anywhere along the slot if you prefer a setting between detents.

To adjust the rear-shock assemblies:

1. Flip the lever of the quick-release pin downward to the unlocked position.
2. Slide the top of the shock assembly left or right along the slot:
   - Move it toward the softer setting for a smoother, more cushioned ride.
   - Move it toward the firmer setting for a stiffer, more responsive ride.
3. Adjust the left and right rear-shock assemblies to the same position. They should match either at the same detent position or at the same location within the slot.
4. Flip the quick-release pin lever upward to the locked position to secure the shock assembly.

<PIC>

Important: Always adjust both rear-shock assemblies symmetrically. Uneven left/right settings can affect ride comfort and machine handling.,['Manual23_37', 'Manual23_38']

To adjust the height of cut on a mower equipped with an electric deck lift, follow these steps:

1. Raise the mower deck by pushing up on the deck-lift switch. The switch is located on the control lever; pushing it upward lifts the deck so you can change the cutting-height setting safely and easily.

<PIC>

2. Set the desired cutting height by moving the height-of-cut pin. Choose the hole in the height-of-cut bracket that matches the cutting height you want, then insert the pin securely into that hole. Make sure the pin is fully seated before lowering the deck.

<PIC>

3. Lower the deck into position by pushing down on the deck-lift switch. The deck will lower until it rests at the height determined by the pin position.

Important: Whenever you change the height of cut, also adjust the anti-scalp rollers to match the new cutting height. This helps prevent the deck from scalping uneven ground and improves cut quality.,['Manual23_55', 'Manual23_56']

To load the lawn mower onto a trailer or truck, please follow the steps below and use extra caution, as loading on a ramp increases the risk of loss of control or tip-over.

1. Prepare the trailer or truck
• Use a heavy-duty trailer or truck suitable for transporting the machine.
• Use a full-width ramp only—do not use separate individual ramps for the left and right sides.
• Make sure the trailer or truck has the required brakes, lighting, and markings according to local regulations.
• Do not drive the mower on public streets or roadways.

Warning: Loading the mower onto a trailer or truck can increase the possibility of tip-over, which may cause serious injury or death.

<PIC>

2. Check the ramp setup
Before loading, confirm the ramp meets these safety requirements:
• The ramp angle must not exceed 15 degrees between the ramp and the ground, or between the ramp and the trailer/truck bed.
• The ramp length should be at least 4 times the height of the trailer or truck bed from the ground. This helps keep the ramp angle within the safe limit.
• The ramp must be stable, secure, and wide enough to support the full width of the machine.

3. Load the mower
1) If using a trailer, connect it securely to the towing vehicle and attach the safety chains.
2) If applicable, connect the trailer brakes and lights.
3) Lower the full-width ramp and verify that the ramp angle is no more than 15 degrees.
4) Back the mower up the ramp onto the trailer or truck. Do not drive forward up the ramp.

<PIC>

Important operating tips while on the ramp:
• Use extreme caution and move slowly.
• Avoid sudden acceleration, sudden braking, or sharp steering movements.
• Back the machine up the ramp for loading, and drive it forward down the ramp when unloading.

4. Secure the mower after loading
Once the mower is positioned on the trailer or truck:
1) Shut off the engine.
2) Remove the key.
3) Engage the parking brake.
4) Tie down the machine near the front caster wheels and the rear bumper using appropriate straps, chains, cables, or ropes.
5) Follow local tie-down requirements to ensure the mower is properly secured for transport.

<PIC>

In short: use a heavy-duty trailer or truck, a full-width ramp with a maximum 15-degree angle, back the mower up the ramp, then shut it off and secure it firmly at the front and rear tie-down points.,['Manual23_63', 'Manual23_64', 'Manual23_65']

To unload the lawn mower safely from a trailer or truck, follow these steps:

1. Lower the full-width ramp and check the ramp angle.
   Make sure the angle between the ramp and the ground does not exceed 15°. The ramp should be long enough to keep the slope gentle—ideally at least 4 times the height of the trailer or truck bed from the ground. Use only a full-width ramp, not separate narrow ramps for each side of the mower.

<PIC>

2. Drive the mower forward down the ramp.
   Keep the machine straight, move slowly, and maintain steady control as you come down the ramp. Do not back down the ramp.

<PIC>

Important safety reminders:
• Use extreme caution whenever operating the mower on a ramp.
• Avoid sudden acceleration, sudden braking, or sharp steering movements while on the ramp, as these can cause loss of control or a tip-over.
• Make sure the ramp and trailer are stable before unloading.
• Keep bystanders clear of the unloading area.,['Manual23_63', 'Manual23_64']

To change the engine oil on your lawn mower, first confirm which engine is installed, because the drain/fill illustrations differ for Kawasaki and Kohler engines.

<PIC>

**Recommended interval:** Change the engine oil every **100 hours** of operation, or more often if you operate in dirty or dusty conditions.

### 1. Warm the oil
Start the engine and let it run for about **5 minutes**. Warm oil drains more easily and helps remove more contaminants from the crankcase.

### 2. Position the mower safely
Park the machine so the **oil-drain side is slightly lower** than the opposite side. This helps the oil drain completely.

### 3. Secure the machine
Disengage the blade-control switch/PTO and engage the parking brake.

### 4. Shut down before servicing
Turn off the engine, remove the key, and wait for all moving parts to stop before leaving the operating position.

### 5. Drain the old engine oil
Place a drain pan under the oil-drain hose, loosen and remove the drain plug, and allow the oil to drain fully. Reinstall and tighten the drain plug securely afterward.

**For Kawasaki engines:**

<PIC>

**For Kohler engines:**

<PIC>

### 6. Add new engine oil
Slowly pour in about **80% of the specified oil capacity** first, then add the remaining oil gradually until the level reaches the **FULL** mark on the dipstick.

Typical oil capacities:
- **Kawasaki FX751 / FX801:** 2.3 L / 78 fl oz with filter change; 2.1 L / 71 fl oz without filter change
- **Kawasaki FX921:** 1.9 L / 64 fl oz with filter change; 1.7 L / 57 fl oz without filter change
- **Kohler engine:** 1.9 L / 64 fl oz with filter change; 1.6 L / 54 fl oz without filter change

When checking the level, wipe the dipstick clean, reinsert it as shown for your engine type, then confirm the oil reaches the **FULL** mark.

**For Kawasaki engines:**

<PIC>

**For Kohler engines:**

<PIC>

### 7. Run and recheck
Start the engine, drive the mower to a flat area, shut it off, and check the oil level again. Add oil only as needed to bring it to the FULL mark.

### Important notes
- Dispose of used oil at a proper recycling center.
- Do not overfill or underfill the engine crankcase, as either condition can damage the engine.
- If you are also replacing the oil filter, drain the oil first, replace the filter, then refill with the correct amount of new oil.,['Manual23_70', 'Manual23_75', 'Manual23_86', 'Manual23_76', 'Manual23_87']

To remove the air filters from your lawn mower, first make sure the machine is completely safe to work on, then follow the steps for the engine type installed on your mower.

**Before you start — safety preparation**

1. Park the mower on a level surface.
2. Disengage the blade-control switch/PTO.
3. Engage the parking brake.
4. Shut off the engine, remove the key, and wait until all moving parts have completely stopped before leaving the operating position or opening the engine area.

---

## For Kawasaki engines

1. Locate the air-cleaner assembly on the engine.
2. Release the latches on the air cleaner.
3. Pull the air-cleaner cover away from the air-cleaner body.

<PIC>

4. Clean the inside of the air-cleaner cover using compressed air.
5. Gently slide the **primary filter** out of the air-cleaner body.
   - Be careful not to knock or scrape the filter against the side of the air-cleaner body, as this can dislodge dirt into the intake area.
6. Remove the **safety filter** only if you are replacing it. Do not remove it just for cleaning.

---

## For Kohler engines

Kohler engines have an air-inlet cover/screen area as well as the main air-cleaner cover, so remove the filters in two stages.

1. Locate the air-cleaner assembly.
2. Release the latches on the air cleaner.
3. Pull the **air-inlet cover** off the air-cleaner body.
4. Clean the air-inlet screen and the cover.
5. Reinstall the air-inlet cover and secure it with the latches.

<PIC>

6. Release the latches on the main air-cleaner cover.
7. Pull the air-cleaner cover off the air-cleaner body.
8. Clean the inside of the air-cleaner cover with compressed air.
9. Gently slide the **primary filter** out of the air-cleaner body.
   - Avoid knocking the filter against the side of the body.

<PIC>

10. Remove the **inner filter** only if you intend to replace it.

---

**Important filter handling notes**

- Do not attempt to clean the safety filter or inner filter. If it is dirty, replace both the inner/safety filter and the primary filter.
- Do not operate the engine with the filters or air-cleaner cover removed, as this can allow dirt into the engine and cause damage.
- When reinstalling filters, make sure the primary filter is fully seated and the cover is securely latched before starting the mower.,['Manual23_72', 'Manual23_82', 'Manual23_83']

To replace the mower belt, follow the steps below. For safety, make sure the machine is completely stopped before working around the deck or belt area.

1. Park the mower on a level surface.
2. Disengage the blade-control switch (PTO) and engage the parking brake.
3. Shut off the engine, remove the key, and wait for all moving parts to stop before leaving the operating position.
4. Lower the mower deck to the 76 mm / 3 inch height-of-cut position.
5. Remove the belt covers. Press the cover tab down, slide the cover out, and remove the covers from the mower deck as shown below.

<PIC>

6. Insert a 3/8-inch ratchet into the square hole in the idler arm to release tension from the idler spring.
7. Remove the belt from the mower-deck pulleys and the clutch pulley.
8. Remove the belt guide from the spring-loaded idler arm.
9. Remove the old mower belt.
10. Route the new belt around the mower-deck pulleys and around the clutch pulley under the engine. Make sure the belt follows the routing shown in the diagram and sits fully in each pulley groove.

<PIC>

11. Reinstall the belt guide on the idler arm.
12. Using the 3/8-inch ratchet in the square hole again, reinstall the idler spring to restore belt tension.

Note: Confirm that both ends of the spring are fully seated in the anchor grooves before continuing.

13. Reinstall the belt covers. Slide each cover into position, press it down, and make sure the locking tab snaps securely into the deck slot.

<PIC>

After installation, double-check that the belt is correctly seated on all pulleys, the spring is properly anchored, the belt guide is installed, and both belt covers are locked in place before operating the mower.,['Manual23_99', 'Manual23_100', 'Manual23_101']

To set up the controls on an over-the-range microwave, use the Control Set-Up pad on the control panel. This lets you adjust several default settings, including the beep sound, clock display, display scroll speed, and defrost weight unit.

For an over-the-range microwave, the Light Timer lets you set the cooktop/countertop light to turn on and off automatically. It turns on at the low light setting at the same time every day, then turns off at your selected time until you reset or cancel it.

Example: set the light to turn on at 2:00 AM and turn off at 7:00 AM.

<PIC>

Steps to set the Light Timer:

1. Touch the Light Timer pad.
   - Press once for AM.
   - Press twice for PM.

2. Enter the time you want the light to turn on.
   - For 2:00, press [2], [0], [0].

3. Touch the Light Timer pad again.
   - Press once for AM.
   - Press twice for PM.

4. Enter the time you want the light to turn off.
   - For 7:00, press [7], [0], [0].

5. Touch Start to save the setting.

To change or reset the Light Timer, repeat the same steps with the new on/off times.

To cancel the Light Timer while it is operating, touch the Light HI/LO/Off pad. If you are in the Light Timer setup screen and want to cancel the setting, touch Stop/Clear.,['Manual24_11']

Yes—on your over-the-range microwave, the Favorite Recipe feature lets you save one cooking instruction in memory so you can quickly start it again later. For example, you can set it to cook for 2 minutes, then recall that same setting next time with just a couple of touches.

To save a Favorite Recipe for 2 minutes:

1. Touch the Favorite Recipe pad. The display will show: “1:30 TOUCH START OR ENTER NEW TIME”.
2. Enter the desired cooking time by touching the number pads [2], [0], and [0]. The display will show: “2:00 TOUCH START OR POWER”.
3. Touch the Start pad. The microwave will begin cooking, and the display will show the time counting down.

To use the saved Favorite Recipe later:

1. Touch the Favorite Recipe pad. The display will show the saved time, for example: “2:00 TOUCH START OR POWER”.
2. Touch the Start pad to begin cooking. The display will count down the cooking time.

Helpful notes:
• When the cooking time ends, the microwave will beep four times and END will appear on the display.
• The power level defaults to High, but you can adjust the power level when setting the recipe if needed.
• The Favorite Recipe feature stores one saved cooking setting at a time; entering a new one will replace the previous saved setting.,['Manual24_16', 'Manual24_17']

To reheat food in an over-the-range microwave, use the Reheat Sensor feature. This feature automatically senses steam/humidity from the food and adjusts the heating time for you, so you do not need to enter a cooking time or power level manually. The Reheat Sensor includes 3 preset food categories: How to reheat food 1. Place the food in a microwave-safe container. 2. Cover it loosely with a microwave-safe lid, waxed paper, or vented plastic wrap. 3. Touch the Reheat pad. 4. Select the food category by pressing the matching number pad: - 1 = Casserole - 2 = Dinner Plate - 3 = Soup/Sauce 5. The display will show the selected food type once, then show “SENSING” while the microwave detects humidity and calculates the remaining reheating time. 6. When reheating is complete, the microwave will beep four times and “END” will appear on the display. Example: Reheating a casserole For a casserole, touch Reheat, then press 1. The display will briefly show “CASSEROLE”, then switch to “SENSING”. Recommended amounts For best reheating results: - Use only microwave-safe cookware, such as ovenproof glass, microwave-safe china without metallic trim, or approved microwave-safe plastic/paper products. - Cover food loosely so steam can escape. Do not use a tight-sealing cover. - Make sure the outside of the container and the inside of the microwave are dry before starting; moisture can affect the sensor. - Do not open the door or press Stop/Clear while the display shows “SENSING”. - After sensing is complete and the remaining time appears, you may open the door if needed to stir, turn, or rearrange the food, then press Start to continue.,['Manual24_29', 'Manual24_30', 'Manual24_31']

Your over-the-range microwave has 3 preset Auto Defrost programs. Auto Defrost is recommended for frozen raw foods because it automatically calculates the defrosting time based on the food type and weight.

First, select the correct food category:

<PIC>

**Auto Defrost categories**
- **1 = Meat**
- **2 = Poultry**
- **3 = Fish**

**Example: Defrosting 1.2 lbs. of ground beef**

<PIC>

Follow these steps:
1. Touch **Auto Defrost**.
2. Select the food category. For ground beef, touch **1** for **Meat**.
3. Enter the food weight in pounds and tenths of a pound. For **1.2 lbs**, touch **1** and **2**.
4. Touch **Start**. The display will begin counting down the defrost time and show **DEFROST**.

**Entering the correct weight**

For Auto Defrost, the weight must be entered in pounds and tenths of a pound, using the net food weight only—not including the container or packaging. If your food package lists ounces, use this conversion chart to enter the correct decimal weight:

<PIC>

For example:
- 8 oz = 0.5 lb
- 12 oz = 0.75 lb
- 16 oz = 1.0 lb

**During the defrost cycle**

The microwave will beep during defrosting. When it beeps:
1. Open the door.
2. Turn, separate, or rearrange the food.
3. Remove any portions that have already thawed.
4. Return the still-frozen portions to the microwave.
5. Touch **Start** again to continue defrosting.

**Helpful defrosting tips**

- Use **Auto Defrost** only for raw frozen food.
- Enter the food’s net weight in pounds and tenths of a pound.
- If the food has been outside the freezer for up to 20 minutes, enter a slightly lower weight to avoid over-defrosting or partially cooking it.
- Separate pieces as they begin to thaw so they defrost more evenly.
- Shallow, rectangular packages defrost faster than thick blocks.
- Small pieces of aluminum foil may be used to shield thin or warm areas, such as chicken wing tips or fish tails, but the foil must not touch the microwave walls.
- For best results, place food on a microwave-safe roasting rack to keep thawed juices from warming or cooking the food during defrosting.
- The available Auto Defrost weight range is **0.1 to 6.0 lbs**.,['Manual24_32', 'Manual24_33', 'Manual24_34']

The grease filter on your over-the-range microwave should be removed and cleaned regularly—at least once a month, or more often if you cook frequently with grease or steam.

1. **Remove the grease filter**  
   From the underside of the microwave, slide the filter to one side. Then pull it downward and push it toward the opposite side until it drops out.

   <PIC>

2. **Clean the filter**  
   Soak the grease filter in hot water with a mild detergent, then gently wash away the grease. Rinse it thoroughly under running water and shake off excess water to help it dry.  
   **Do not use ammonia or harsh/corrosive cleaners.** Avoid abrasive pads, as they may damage the aluminum mesh. If the aluminum filter is exposed to harsh cleaning methods, it may darken.

   <PIC>

3. **Reinstall the filter**  
   Once the filter is fully dry, slide it back into the side slot. Then push it upward and toward the center of the microwave until it locks securely into place.

   <PIC>

**Caution:** Do not operate the microwave hood/vent without the grease filter installed, as this may cause personal injury or property damage.,['Manual24_47', 'Manual24_48', 'Manual24_49']

If your over-the-range microwave is set up to recirculate air back into the room, the charcoal filter should be replaced every 6 to 12 months, or more often if you cook frequently or notice lingering odors. Please note that the charcoal filter cannot be washed or reused.

Before you begin, make sure the microwave is completely powered off.

1. Disconnect power
Unplug the microwave, or turn off power at the main power supply/circuit breaker. This helps prevent electric shock while you are working near the vent area.

2. Remove the vent grill screws
At the top front of the microwave, locate the two middle mounting screws that secure the vent grill. Remove these two screws.

<PIC>

3. Remove the vent grill
After the screws are removed, gently tip the vent grill forward, then lift it out and set it aside.

4. Take out the old charcoal filter
With the grill removed, you will see the charcoal filter inside the top opening. Pull the old filter out from its slot.

<PIC>

5. Install the new charcoal filter
Slide the new charcoal filter into the same position. Make sure it rests at the angled position shown, rather than lying flat. This angle helps the filter sit correctly in the airflow path.

<PIC>

6. Reinstall the vent grill
Slide the bottom edge of the vent grill back into place first. Then push the top of the grill inward until it snaps into position. Reinstall the two mounting screws.

7. Restore power
Turn the power back on at the main power supply or plug the microwave back in. If the display has reset, set the clock again.

That’s it—the charcoal filter replacement is complete.,['Manual24_50', 'Manual24_51', 'Manual24_52']

To replace the oven light in your over-the-range microwave, please follow these steps carefully:

1. **Disconnect the power first.** Unplug the microwave, or turn off power at the main power supply/circuit breaker. This is required before replacing the bulb.

2. **Remove the vent cover mounting screws.** Locate and remove the two middle screws securing the vent cover at the top front of the microwave.

3. **Remove the vent cover.** Tip the cover forward, then lift it out to remove it.

<PIC>

4. **Remove the bulb holder.** After the vent cover is removed, locate the bulb holder and remove the screw/fastener holding it in place.

<PIC>

5. **Lift up the bulb holder.** Carefully lift and pull the bulb holder forward so you can access the bulb.

<PIC>

6. **Replace the bulb.** Remove the old bulb and install a new **30-watt or 40-watt appliance bulb**.

7. **Reinstall the bulb holder.** Place the bulb holder back into position and secure it.

8. **Reinstall the vent cover.** Slide the bottom of the vent cover into place first, then push the top until it snaps into place. Reinstall the mounting screws.

9. **Restore power.** Turn the power back on at the main power supply, or plug the microwave back in.

**Safety note:** To avoid electrical shock, make sure the power is disconnected before replacing the bulb. Do not remove the outer case of the microwave; that should only be handled by an authorized service provider.,['Manual24_54', 'Manual24_55', 'Manual24_56']

The motherboard provides three PCI Express 3.0 x16 physical slots for PCIe graphics cards. The actual lane mode is determined automatically by how many VGA/PCIe cards are installed and which slots are populated.

<PIC>

Recommended slot setup:

• Single VGA/PCIe card: install the graphics card in PCIe 3.0 x16_1, the gray slot. This slot runs at x16 and is recommended for the best single-card performance.

• Dual VGA/PCIe cards: install the cards in PCIe 3.0 x16_1 and PCIe 3.0 x16_2. These two slots will operate at x8/x8.

• Triple VGA/PCIe cards: install the cards in PCIe 3.0 x16_1, PCIe 3.0 x16_2, and PCIe 3.0 x16_3. In this configuration, PCIe 3.0 x16_1 and PCIe 3.0 x16_2 operate at x8/x8, while PCIe 3.0 x16_3 operates at x4.

If you need to manually configure the PCIe link speed in BIOS, enter BIOS and go to:
Advanced > System Agent (SA) Configuration > PEG Port Configuration > PCIEx16_1 / PCIEx16_2 Link Speed

Available options are:
[Auto] [Gen1] [Gen2] [Gen3]

In most cases, we recommend keeping this setting on [Auto] unless you are troubleshooting compatibility with a specific graphics card or PCIe device.

Additional notes:
• Before installing or removing any expansion card, shut down the system and unplug the power cord.
• When using multiple graphics cards, make sure your power supply provides sufficient wattage and the required PCIe power connectors.
• For better cooling in multi-GPU configurations, connect chassis fans to the CHA_FAN1/CHA_FAN2/CHA_FAN3 headers and ensure good airflow inside the case.,['Manual25_31']

Before setting any motherboard jumpers, please power off the computer completely and unplug the power cord from the wall outlet. To reduce the risk of static damage, touch a grounded metal object or use an anti-static wrist strap before handling the motherboard.

There are two jumper-related settings on this motherboard:

### 1. Clear RTC RAM jumper — 2-pin CLRTC

The **CLRTC** header is used to clear the Real Time Clock / CMOS data. This resets stored system setup information such as date, time, BIOS settings, and system passwords.

<PIC>

To clear the RTC RAM:

1. Turn off the computer and unplug the power cord.
2. Locate the **CLRTC** 2-pin header on the motherboard.
3. Use a metal object, such as a screwdriver, to short the two pins.
4. Reconnect the power cord and turn on the computer.
5. During startup, press and hold the **Del** key to enter BIOS setup, then re-enter or adjust the required settings.

Additional notes:

- If the system still does not recover after the steps above, remove the onboard button cell battery, short the two CLRTC pins again, then reinstall the battery after clearing CMOS.
- If the system fails because of overclocking, you usually do not need to clear RTC manually. Shut down and reboot the system; the BIOS can automatically reset the related parameters to default values through CPU Parameter Recall.

### 2. CPU Over Voltage jumper — 3-pin CPU_OV

The **CPU_OV** jumper controls whether higher CPU voltage options are available. This is mainly intended for advanced overclocking and depends on the installed CPU.

<PIC>

CPU_OV jumper settings:

- **Pins 1–2 shorted:** CPU Over Voltage disabled — this is the default setting.
- **Pins 2–3 shorted:** CPU Over Voltage enabled — allows access to higher CPU voltage settings.

Please be careful when changing CPU voltage or overclocking-related settings. Incorrect jumper placement or excessive voltage settings may cause system instability, boot failure, or hardware malfunction.,['Manual25_33', 'Manual25_34']

You can set up the motherboard rear panel connectors by plugging each device into the matching port on the rear I/O panel. Please power off the system before connecting or removing major peripherals, and make sure each connector is aligned correctly before inserting it.

<PIC>

Rear panel connector functions:

1. PS/2 Mouse/Keyboard combo port: Connects to a PS/2 mouse or PS/2 keyboard.
2. DisplayPort: Connects to a DisplayPort-compatible monitor or display device.
3. VGA port: Connects to a VGA monitor or other VGA-compatible display device.
4. LAN (RJ-45) port: Connects the computer to a Gigabit Local Area Network through a network cable, router, switch, or network hub.

The LAN port has two indicator LEDs that help you check the network connection status:

<PIC>

LAN port LED indications:

<PIC>

- Activity/Link LED: Shows whether the network cable is linked and whether data is being transmitted.
- Speed LED: Shows the current network speed, such as 10 Mbps, 100 Mbps, or 1 Gbps.

5. Center/Subwoofer port, orange: Connects the center speaker or subwoofer in multi-channel speaker setups.
6. Rear Speaker Out port, black: Connects rear speakers for 4.1-channel, 5.1-channel, or 7.1-channel audio configurations.
7. Line In port, light blue: Connects external audio sources such as a tape player, CD player, DVD player, or other audio input devices.
8. Line Out port, lime: Connects headphones or speakers. In 4.1-channel, 5.1-channel, and 7.1-channel configurations, this port functions as Front Speaker Out.
9. Microphone port, pink: Connects a microphone.

For different audio setups, use the audio ports according to the channel configuration shown below:

<PIC>

10. Optical S/PDIF out port: Connects to amplified speakers, headphones, or S/PDIF-compatible digital audio devices.
11. USB 3.1 Type-A port: Connects USB 3.1 Type-A devices.
12. USB 3.1 Type-C port: Connects USB 3.1 Type-C mobile devices or peripherals.
13. USB 3.0 ports 3–6: Connect USB 3.0 devices. For best transfer speed and stability, connect USB 3.0 devices to these USB 3.0 ports.
14. DVI-D port: Connects DVI-D-compatible display devices.
15. HDMI port: Connects HDMI-compatible monitors, TVs, or other display devices. It supports HDCP playback for protected HD content.
16. USB 2.0 ports 13 and 14: Connect USB 2.0 or USB 1.1 devices such as keyboards, mice, printers, or other peripherals.

Additional notes:
- USB 3.0 devices are recommended to be connected to USB 3.0 ports for faster performance.
- DVI-D does not output an RGB analog signal to CRT displays and is not compatible with DVI-I conversion.
- If you use multiple display outputs, the actual supported display combination may depend on the CPU graphics capability, operating system, and display settings.,['Manual25_35', 'Manual25_36', 'Manual25_37', 'Manual25_38']

You can configure the motherboard’s onboard lighting either in the BIOS or through the LED Control app in Ai Suite 3. The configurable onboard LEDs include the Model name LEDs and the SupremeFX LED.

Please note: the Standby Power LED is a status indicator. It lights up when the system is powered on, in sleep mode, or in soft-off mode, and it is not used for lighting-effect customization.

1. Model name LEDs

The Model name LEDs are located under the printed motherboard model name. You can turn them off or change their lighting behavior.

<PIC>

To change the Model name LED setting in BIOS:

Advanced > Onboard Devices Configuration > Model Name LED Lighting

Available options include:
- Disabled: turns off the Model name LEDs
- Still Mode: keeps the LEDs continuously lit
- Breathing Mode: makes the LEDs blink/fade intermittently

<PIC>

2. SupremeFX LED

The SupremeFX LED is located along the audio separation path on the motherboard. It can also be turned off or set to different lighting effects.

<PIC>

To change the SupremeFX LED setting in BIOS:

Advanced > Onboard Devices Configuration > SupremeFX LED Lighting

Available options include:
- Disabled: turns off the SupremeFX LED
- Still Mode: keeps the LED continuously lit
- Breathing Mode: makes the LED blink/fade intermittently
- Flowing Mode: creates a flowing lighting effect

<PIC>

After selecting the desired mode in BIOS, save your changes and restart the system for the setting to take effect.,['Manual25_55', 'Manual25_56', 'Manual25_57', 'Manual25_58']

You can update the motherboard BIOS using one of the following methods: EZ Update, EZ Flash 3, or BIOS Updater in DOS. Before starting, we recommend saving a copy of the current BIOS file to a USB flash drive in case you need to restore it later.

Important precautions before updating:
• Download the latest BIOS file for your exact motherboard model.
• Do not shut down, restart, or remove power during the BIOS update process, as this may cause the system to fail to boot.
• If using a USB flash drive, format it as FAT32 or FAT16, and use a single partition.
• After updating, load the default BIOS settings for better compatibility and stability. You can do this from the BIOS Exit menu by selecting Load Optimized Defaults.

1. Update BIOS with EZ Update

EZ Update is a Windows-based utility in AI Suite 3. It can automatically update motherboard software, drivers, firmware, and BIOS. You can also manually select a downloaded BIOS file and update it from within Windows.

To use EZ Update:
1. Open AI Suite 3.
2. Click EZ Update on the main menu bar.
3. To check online for updates, click Check Now.
4. To update using a BIOS file you already downloaded, click the browse button “...” and select the BIOS file.
5. Click Update to start the BIOS update.

<PIC>

Note: EZ Update requires an active Internet connection if you want it to check for updates online.

2. Update BIOS with EZ Flash 3

EZ Flash 3 lets you update the BIOS directly from the BIOS interface, without using a Windows-based utility. You can update either from a USB flash drive or through the Internet.

<PIC>

To update the BIOS using EZ Flash 3:
1. Enter the BIOS setup interface.
2. Switch to Advanced Mode. You can press F7 if needed.
3. Go to the Tool menu.
4. Select EZ Flash 3 Utility and press Enter.
5. Choose one of the update methods: by USB or by Internet.

Via USB:
1. Copy the latest BIOS file to a FAT32/FAT16 single-partition USB flash drive.
2. Insert the USB flash drive into a USB port.
3. In EZ Flash 3, select by USB.
4. Press Tab to switch to the Drive field.
5. Use the Up/Down arrow keys to select the USB flash drive, then press Enter.
6. Press Tab to switch to the Folder Info field.
7. Select the BIOS file and press Enter to start the update.
8. Reboot the system after the update is complete.

Via the Internet:
1. In EZ Flash 3, select by Internet.
2. Use the Left/Right arrow keys to choose the Internet connection method, then press Enter.
3. Follow the onscreen instructions to complete the update.
4. Reboot the system after the update is complete.

Note: Please check that the Internet connection is stable before updating through the Internet.

3. Update BIOS with BIOS Updater in DOS

BIOS Updater allows you to update the BIOS in a DOS environment. This method requires a USB flash drive and an optical drive.

Before updating:
• Prepare the motherboard support DVD and a USB flash drive.
• Download the latest BIOS file and BIOS Updater, then save both to the USB flash drive.
• Make sure the USB flash drive is FAT32/FAT16 and single-partition. NTFS is not supported in FreeDOS.
• Turn off the computer before preparing to boot into DOS.
• Make sure the computer has a DVD optical drive.

Boot into DOS:
1. Insert the USB flash drive containing the latest BIOS file and BIOS Updater into a USB port.
2. Start the computer and press F8 during POST to open the boot device selection screen.
3. Insert the support DVD into the optical drive.
4. Select the optical drive as the boot device.

<PIC>

5. When the boot message appears, press Enter within five seconds to enter the FreeDOS prompt.
6. At the FreeDOS prompt, type the following command and press Enter:
   d:

Update the BIOS file:
1. At the FreeDOS prompt, type the following command and press Enter:
   bupdater /pc /g
2. On the BIOS Updater screen, press Tab to switch from the Files panel to the Drives panel.
3. Select D: as the drive.

<PIC>

4. Press Tab again to switch from the Drives panel to the Files panel.
5. Use the arrow keys to select the BIOS file, then press Enter.
6. After BIOS Updater checks the selected file, select Yes to confirm the BIOS update.

<PIC>

7. Select Yes again and press Enter to proceed.
8. When the update is complete, press Esc to exit BIOS Updater.
9. Restart the computer.

After the BIOS update:
• Enter BIOS again and load the default settings.
• Select Load Optimized Defaults under the Exit menu.
• Save the changes and restart the system.

If you are not sure which method to choose, EZ Flash 3 via USB is generally recommended because it updates directly from the BIOS environment and does not depend on the operating system.,['Manual25_60', 'Manual25_61', 'Manual25_62', 'Manual25_63', 'Manual25_64']

Yes — you can create a RAID array using the motherboard’s onboard Intel chipset. This motherboard supports RAID 0, RAID 1, RAID 5, and RAID 10 through the Intel SATA 6.0 Gb/s connectors.

Before you start, please note:
- Back up any important data first. Creating RAID may delete or affect existing data on the drives.
- Use SATA drives connected to the Intel SATA connectors, not other storage controllers.
- The SATA mode is set to AHCI by default, so it must be changed to RAID before creating the array.
- Make sure the drives you want to use do not already contain existing RAID volumes.

1. Connect the SATA hard drives to the Intel SATA 6.0 Gb/s ports on the motherboard.

<PIC>

These Intel SATA ports can be used to create RAID 0, 1, 5, or 10. If you are using these ports for RAID, enter BIOS and set SATA Mode Selection to RAID instead of AHCI.

2. Enter BIOS Setup. From the EZ Mode screen, press F11 on your keyboard, or click EZ Tuning Wizard.

<PIC>

3. In EZ Tuning Wizard, select RAID, then click Yes to enable RAID mode.

<PIC>

Please confirm the following before continuing:
- The HDDs/SSDs are connected to Intel SATA connectors.
- The drives do not contain an existing RAID volume.

4. The system will display the available drives. Confirm the listed drives are the ones you want to use, then click Next.

<PIC>

5. Select the RAID storage purpose, then click Next:
- Easy Backup: focuses on data redundancy/protection.
- Super Speed: focuses on performance.

<PIC>

6. If you choose Easy Backup, select one of the following RAID types, then click Next:
- Easy Backup (RAID 1): mirrors data across two drives for redundancy.
- Easy Backup (RAID 10): combines mirroring and striping, but requires four drives.

<PIC>

Note: RAID 10 is only available when four HDDs/SSDs are connected.

7. If you choose Super Speed, select one of the following RAID types, then click Next:
- Super Speed (RAID 0): improves performance by striping data, but does not provide redundancy.
- Super Speed (RAID 5): provides a balance of performance and fault tolerance, and requires at least three drives.

<PIC>

8. After selecting the RAID type, click Yes to continue creating the RAID array.

9. When the RAID setup is complete, click Yes to exit the setup, then click OK to restart the system.

After the restart, you can continue with OS installation or driver setup as needed. If the operating system will be installed onto the RAID array, make sure the required Intel RAID/RST driver is available during installation if prompted.,['Manual25_48', 'Manual25_65', 'Manual25_71', 'Manual25_72', 'Manual25_73', 'Manual25_74', 'Manual25_75']

To install OS 7 on a 100 Series motherboard using a SATA optical drive and a USB device, you can load the USB 3.0 driver from the support DVD while starting the OS installation from the USB device.

Before starting, please prepare the following:

• 1 x support DVD
• 1 x OS 7 installation source
• 1 x SATA optical disk drive / SATA ODD
• 1 x USB device, such as a USB ODD or USB storage device

Note: If you use a USB storage device, it should have at least 8 GB capacity. We recommend formatting the USB storage device before copying the installation files, and backing up any important data first.

Steps:

1. Prepare the OS 7 installation source.
   - If you are using an OS 7 installation DVD, insert it into a USB ODD.
   - If you are using a USB storage device, copy all files from the OS 7 installation DVD to the USB storage device on another working computer.

2. Connect the USB ODD or USB storage device containing the OS 7 installation files to the 100 Series motherboard system.

3. Insert the support DVD into the SATA ODD connected to the same 100 Series motherboard system. This disc provides the USB 3.0 driver needed during OS 7 installation.

4. Power on the system. During POST, press F8 to open the boot device selection screen.

<PIC>

5. In the boot menu, select the USB ODD or USB storage device that contains the OS 7 installation files, then press Enter to boot from it.

6. During the installation startup process, the USB 3.0 driver will be loaded automatically from the support DVD in the SATA ODD. When the driver is loaded correctly, the installation screen will continue and display “Setup is starting...”.

<PIC>

7. After the “Setup is starting...” screen appears, continue following the on-screen instructions to complete the OS 7 installation.

If the USB device does not appear in the boot menu, please confirm that the USB device is connected properly, the USB installation files were copied correctly, and USB support is enabled in BIOS.,['Manual25_87', 'Manual25_88']

To secure the motherboard to the chassis, please follow these steps:

1. **Power off and disconnect the system first**  
   Before installing or removing the motherboard, unplug the power cord from the power outlet to avoid injury or damage to components.

2. **Place the motherboard in the correct orientation**  
   Position the motherboard inside the chassis so that the edge with the external I/O ports faces the rear of the chassis. This allows the ports to align properly with the rear I/O opening.

3. **Align the screw holes**  
   Make sure the motherboard mounting holes line up with the chassis standoffs. The diagram below shows the nine screw-hole positions marked with red circles.

<PIC>

4. **Install the screws**  
   Insert and tighten **nine screws** into the circled screw holes to secure the motherboard firmly to the chassis.

**Important:** Do not over-tighten the screws, as excessive force may damage the motherboard. Tighten them only until the board is securely held in place.,['Manual25_6']

To prepare for installing the Central Processing Unit (CPU) on this motherboard, first confirm that you are using a compatible processor and handle the CPU socket carefully.

<PIC>

This motherboard uses a surface-mounted LGA1151 CPU socket, designed for 6th Generation processors. Before installing the CPU, please check the following important points:

• Disconnect all power cables before touching or installing any component.
• Use only a CPU designed for the LGA1151 socket. Do not install CPUs made for LGA1150, LGA1155, or LGA1156 sockets.
• Before installation, check that the PnP protective cap is still on the CPU socket and that the socket contacts are not bent or damaged.
• If the PnP cap is missing, or if you notice any damage to the socket contacts or motherboard components, contact your retailer before proceeding.
• Keep the socket protective cap after installation. It may be required if the motherboard needs to be returned for service.
• Avoid touching the socket pins or CPU contact area, and hold the CPU by its edges to prevent damage from static electricity or contamination.

To install the CPU, follow these steps carefully:

<PIC>

1. Press the load lever down, then move it outward to release it from the retention tab. Make sure you open the socket using the correct lever direction, as shown in the correct illustration.

<PIC>

2. Open the CPU socket cover.
3. Align the triangle orientation mark on the CPU with the triangle mark on the CPU installation tool. Correct alignment is essential, because the CPU must be placed into the socket in only one direction.

<PIC>

4. Secure the CPU into the CPU installation tool until it clicks firmly into place.
5. Hold both sides of the CPU installation tool and gently place the CPU into the motherboard socket.
6. Lower the load plate, then secure the load lever back into position.
7. Remove the socket’s protective cover as shown after the CPU is secured.

Additional reminders:

• Make sure the CPU is firmly seated before locking it into the socket.
• Use the CPU installation tool only for CPU installation. Do not bend or damage the tool.
• Always hold both sides of the CPU installation tool when installing, removing, or picking it up.
• Incorrect CPU orientation, forced installation, improper removal, or damage caused by careless handling may damage the CPU socket or motherboard.,['Manual25_9', 'Manual25_10', 'Manual25_11', 'Manual25_12']

You can check the system memory information for this motherboard in two ways: first, by confirming the supported memory specifications and slot layout; second, by checking the installed memory in BIOS.

1. Memory specifications supported by the motherboard

This motherboard provides 4 x DDR4 DIMM slots and supports up to 64 GB of system memory. Supported memory frequency starts from DDR4 2133 MHz and can reach up to DDR4 3400 MHz under overclocking conditions, depending on the CPU and memory modules used.

<PIC>

Key memory support information:
- Memory type: DDR4
- Slots: 4 x DIMM
- Maximum capacity: 64 GB
- Supported module capacities: 2 GB, 4 GB, 8 GB, and 16 GB
- Memory type supported: unbuffered, non-ECC DDR4 DIMMs
- Frequency range: DDR4 2133 MHz up to DDR4 3400 MHz (O.C.)

2. Memory slot layout on the motherboard

The motherboard has four DDR4 DIMM sockets. They are labeled as DIMM_A1, DIMM_A2, DIMM_B1, and DIMM_B2. The slot names are important because using the recommended slots can improve compatibility and allow dual-channel operation.

<PIC>

Please note that DDR4 memory has a different notch position from DDR, DDR2, and DDR3 modules, so DDR/DDR2/DDR3 memory cannot be installed into these DDR4 slots.

3. Recommended memory installation configurations

For better compatibility and stability, install the memory modules according to the recommended population order:

- 1 memory module: install it in DIMM_A2
- 2 memory modules: install them in DIMM_A2 and DIMM_B2
- 4 memory modules: install all four slots, DIMM_A1, DIMM_A2, DIMM_B1, and DIMM_B2

<PIC>

Additional compatibility suggestions:
- For dual-channel operation, use matched memory modules when possible.
- It is recommended to use memory modules with the same CAS Latency.
- For best compatibility, use modules from the same vendor and the same version or production batch when possible.
- If using 4 DIMMs or overclocked memory, ensure good system airflow and memory cooling.

4. How to check the installed system memory in BIOS

To see the actual installed memory, enter BIOS Setup and switch to Advanced Mode. On the Main menu, you can view the basic system information, including the detected memory information.

<PIC>

You can also check more detailed DIMM information through the BIOS SPD Information section, where you can select a specific slot such as DIMM_A1, DIMM_A2, DIMM_B1, or DIMM_B2 to view the memory module information detected in that slot.

Important notes:
- Do not install DDR, DDR2, or DDR3 modules into the DDR4 DIMM slots.
- DIMM voltage below 1.65 V is recommended to help protect the CPU.
- If you install 4 GB or more memory, a 64-bit operating system is recommended so the system can use the memory effectively.
- The actual memory frequency may depend on the CPU, memory module SPD/XMP profile, and BIOS settings. Some high-frequency memory may run at a lower default speed until the memory frequency or XMP profile is configured.,['Manual25_2', 'Manual25_18', 'Manual25_19', 'Manual25_78']

The Serial port connector, marked as COM, is an internal 10-1 pin header on the motherboard used to add a legacy serial/COM port to the system. As shown above, the COM header is located along the bottom edge of the motherboard. To use it, connect a compatible serial port module cable to this header, then install the module’s DB-9 serial port bracket into an available slot opening at the rear of the chassis. The COM header pin assignments include: - DCD - RXD - TXD - DTR - GND - DSR - RTS - CTS - RI Please note that the COM port module is purchased separately; it is not part of the onboard rear I/O ports by default. You can also enable or configure the serial port in BIOS: Advanced > Onboard Devices Configuration > Serial Port Configuration Available settings include: - Serial Port: [On] / [Off] - Change Settings: - [IO=3F8h; IRQ=4] - [IO=2F8h; IRQ=3] - [IO=3E8h; IRQ=4] - [IO=2E8h; IRQ=3] In short, this connector allows you to add a traditional COM/RS-232 serial port for devices that still require serial communication, such as industrial equipment, POS devices, debugging tools, or older peripherals.,['Manual25_39']

The 14-1 pin TPM connector on this motherboard is used to connect a Trusted Platform Module (TPM). A TPM helps securely store encryption keys, digital certificates, passwords, and other sensitive data. It can also improve system and network security by helping protect digital identities and verify platform integrity.

<PIC>

In the diagram, the TPM header is shown on the motherboard layout and enlarged on the right with its pin assignments. The 14-1 pin design means one pin position is intentionally missing/keyed, helping you align the TPM module correctly.

The pin layout is as follows:

- Top row: F_CLKRUN, F_SERIRQ, F_FRAME#, F_LAD3, F_LAD2, F_LAD1, F_LAD0 — with PIN 1 marked at F_LAD0
- Bottom row: +3VSB, S_PCIRST#_TBD, key space, GND, C_PCICLK_TPM, +3V, +3V

When connecting a TPM module, please match the module orientation with the missing/keyed pin position and the PIN 1 marking shown in the diagram. Do not force the module into the connector if it does not align.

Important safety note: before installing or removing the TPM module or any other internal component, turn off the computer, switch off the power supply if applicable, and unplug the power cord. This helps prevent damage to the motherboard or connected components.,['Manual25_40']

The Thermal Sensor connector (2-pin T_SENSOR) is used to connect a thermistor cable, so the motherboard can monitor the temperature of a specific area or component inside the system, such as a chipset area, VRM area, graphics card vicinity, storage device area, or another heat-sensitive location you choose.

<PIC>

For the ASUS Z170 PRO GAMING motherboard, this connector is labeled **T_SENSOR1** on the board. It is a 2-pin header with the following pin definition:

- **Pin 1: SENSOR IN** — temperature signal input from the thermistor cable
- **Pin 2: GND** — ground

Please note that the thermistor cable is not included by default and needs to be purchased separately if you want to use this function.

After connecting the thermistor cable, you can place the sensor end near the component or area you want to monitor. Before connecting or removing the cable, please shut down the system and unplug the power cable to avoid electrical damage.

You can check the detected temperature in BIOS through the **Monitor** menu. The hardware monitor can display several temperature readings, including CPU Temperature, MotherBoard Temperature, VRM Temperature, PCH Temperature, and **T_Sensor Temperature**. If you do not want a specific temperature item to be displayed or monitored, you can set it to **[Ignore]**.

<PIC>

This sensor can also be used with fan control. In BIOS Q-Fan settings, chassis fans or extension fans can use **[T_Sensor]** as the temperature source. This means the fan speed can automatically adjust based on the external thermal sensor reading, which is useful if you want case airflow to respond to the temperature of a specific device or area rather than only the CPU or motherboard temperature.,['Manual25_43', 'Manual25_83']

To set Quick Release (QR/QPR) on your multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer, use the pressure cooking lid and follow these steps:

1. Once the pressure cooking program has finished, press the quick release button down until it clicks and locks into the Vent position.

<PIC>

The quick release button controls the steam release valve. When the button is popped up, it is in the Seal position and pressure is kept inside the cooker. When the button is pushed down, it is in the Vent position and steam can release.

<PIC>

2. After the button is locked in the Vent position, steam will release from the top of the steam release valve. A strong jet of steam is normal during Quick Release.

<PIC>

3. Wait until all pressure has been released. You’ll know it is safe to open the lid when the float valve drops down into the lid. Do not open the lid while the float valve is still raised.

Important safety notes:
- The steam released from the steam release valve is very hot. Keep hands, face, and exposed skin away from the valve while releasing pressure.
- Do not cover or block the steam release valve.
- Do not use Quick Release for fatty, oily, thick, or high-starch foods, such as stews, chilis, pasta, oatmeal, or congee.
- Do not use Quick Release for foods that expand during cooking, such as beans and grains. For these foods, use Natural Release instead.
- Never force the lid open. Only open it after the float valve has dropped.,['multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_01', 'Manual30_14', 'multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_02']

To set the pressure cooking lid on your multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer, use the pressure cooking lid—not the air fryer lid—and lock it onto the cooker base as follows:

<PIC>

**Closing and setting the pressure cooking lid**

1. **Position the lid correctly**  
   Hold the lid by the handle and align the **open/unlock position marker** on the lid with the matching symbol on the cooker base.

2. **Lower the lid onto the track**  
   Once the symbols are aligned, place the lid down evenly so it sits properly on the cooker base track.

3. **Turn the lid clockwise to lock**  
   Rotate the lid clockwise until the **closed/lock symbol** on the lid lines up with the matching symbol on the cooker base. This indicates the lid is properly closed for pressure cooking.

<PIC>

**What to expect after closing the lid**

- The pressure cooking lid automatically sets to **Seal** when it is closed correctly.
- The cooker will play a jingle when the lid is opened or closed.
- The lid can be removed during non-pressure cooking, but once the cooker becomes pressurized, the lid will lock in place automatically.

**Important safety reminder**

Do **not** try to remove the lid while the float valve is raised. Never force the lid open. Wait until all pressure has been released and the float valve has dropped before opening the lid.,['Manual30_11', 'Manual30_13']

To install/set the float valve on your multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer, please use the pressure cooking lid and follow these steps:

1. From the top of the lid, insert the narrow end of the float valve into the float valve hole. The flat end should remain on the top side of the lid.

<PIC>

2. Hold one finger on the flat top of the float valve so it stays in place, then carefully turn the lid over. On the underside of the lid, firmly push the silicone cap onto the bottom end of the float valve until it is securely attached.

<PIC>

3. Before pressure cooking, check that both parts are installed correctly: the float valve should move freely up and down, and the silicone cap should be securely fitted on the underside of the lid.

Important: Do not operate the pressure cooker if the float valve or silicone cap is missing, loose, or not properly installed. The float valve and silicone cap work together to seal in pressure during cooking. Also, never try to open the lid while the float valve is raised; wait until it drops back down before opening the cooker.,['Manual30_36', 'Manual30_37']

The anti-block shield is a safety and pressure-control part located on the underside of the pressure cooking lid. It sits over the steam release pipe and helps prevent food particles, foam, or debris from entering and clogging the pipe during pressure cooking. This supports proper pressure regulation and safe steam release.

<PIC>

Here is the anti-block shield and steam release pipe area shown more closely:

<PIC>

Why it matters:
- It helps keep the steam release pipe clear.
- It supports normal pressure build-up and release.
- It is required for pressure cooking and is an important safety component.
- The cooker should not be operated in pressure-cooking mode without the anti-block shield installed.

Before using the pressure cooking lid, please check these parts for clogging or food residue:
- Steam release valve
- Steam release pipe
- Anti-block shield
- Float valve

For best performance and safety, clean the pressure cooking lid and its removable parts after each use, including the anti-block shield. Always unplug the cooker and allow it to cool to room temperature before cleaning.

<PIC>

To remove the anti-block shield:
1. Hold the pressure cooking lid firmly, as if gripping a steering wheel.
2. Use your thumbs to press firmly against the side of the anti-block shield.
3. Press toward the side of the lid and upward until the shield pops off the prongs underneath.

<PIC>

To reinstall the anti-block shield:
1. Place the anti-block shield back over the prongs.
2. Press down until it snaps securely into position.
3. Confirm it is seated properly before pressure cooking.

<PIC>

Important: Do not attempt to pressure cook without the anti-block shield installed, and do not cover or block the steam release valve or anti-block shield during use.,['Manual30_4', 'Manual30_19', 'Manual30_29', 'Manual30_34', 'Manual30_35']

The steam release valve on this multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer is controlled by the quick release button on the pressure cooking lid. You do not set the valve by turning the valve itself; instead, use the quick release button to switch between Seal and Vent.

<PIC>

To set the steam release valve:

1. Seal position — for pressure cooking or Natural Release
Leave the quick release button popped up. In this position, the cooker can build and hold pressure. After cooking, if your recipe calls for Natural Release, keep the button in this popped-up Seal position and allow the pressure to come down on its own.

<PIC>

2. Vent position — for Quick Release
Press the quick release button down until it clicks and locks into the Vent position. Use this only when your recipe calls for Quick Release. Steam will release from the steam release valve, which is normal.

<PIC>

Important safety tips:
- The lid may say “Turn to Seal,” but the motion is more like a quick flick. Do not turn the quick release button more than 45°, and allow it to spring back to its original position.
- Keep hands, face, and exposed skin away from the steam release valve when releasing pressure, as the steam is very hot.
- Do not cover, block, or place anything over the steam release valve.
- Do not try to open the lid until the float valve has dropped down into the lid. This means the pressure has been fully released.
- For thick, oily, foamy, high-starch foods, or foods that expand during cooking, such as stews, pasta, congee, beans, and grains, Natural Release is usually safer than Quick Release.,['Manual30_14', 'Manual30_9', 'multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_01']

The anti-block shield is located on the underside of the pressure cooking lid. Its job is to help stop food particles from entering the steam release pipe, which supports proper pressure regulation during pressure cooking. It is an essential safety part and must be installed before using any pressure-cooking function.

<PIC>

To install the anti-block shield:

1. Turn the pressure cooking lid over so you can see the underside of the lid.
2. Locate the steam release pipe and the small metal prongs around it.
3. Position the anti-block shield over the prongs.
4. Press the shield straight down firmly until it snaps securely into place.
5. Check that it is seated evenly and does not fall off when the lid is moved.

<PIC>

Please do not operate the pressure cooker without the anti-block shield properly installed, as it is needed for safe pressure cooking and correct steam release performance.,['Manual30_19', 'Manual30_35']

To install the float valve and silicone cap on the pressure cooking lid, please follow these steps:

1. From the top of the pressure cooking lid, insert the narrow end of the float valve down into the float valve hole. The flat end of the float valve should remain visible on the top of the lid.

<PIC>

2. Keep one finger gently pressed on the flat top of the float valve so it does not fall out, then carefully turn the lid over to access the underside.

3. On the bottom side of the lid, firmly push the silicone cap onto the bottom end of the float valve until it is securely attached. This cap helps the float valve seal properly during pressure cooking.

<PIC>

4. After installation, check that the float valve can move up and down freely. When the cooker is pressurized, the float valve rises; when pressure has fully released, it drops back down and sits flush with the lid.

Important: Do not use the pressure cooking function unless both the float valve and silicone cap are correctly installed in the pressure cooking lid. Also, never try to open the lid while the float valve is raised.,['Manual30_36', 'Manual30_37']

To install the condensation collector on your multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer, follow these steps:

1. Locate the slot area at the back of the cooker base, where the condensation collector attaches.
2. Hold the condensation collector so that its grooves line up with the tabs on the back of the cooker base.
3. Slide the condensation collector straight into place until it sits securely against the cooker base.

<PIC>

Please make sure the condensation collector is installed before cooking, as it catches overflow from the condensation rim. After each use, remove it, empty any collected liquid, rinse it out, and allow it to dry before reinstalling.,['Manual30_38']

To install the sealing ring on your multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer, please use the pressure cooking lid and follow these steps:

1. Turn the pressure cooking lid over so you can see the underside of the lid.
2. Locate the circular stainless-steel sealing ring rack.
3. Place the silicone sealing ring over the sealing ring rack.

<PIC>

4. Press the sealing ring firmly into place all the way around the rack. Make sure it sits evenly and is tucked securely behind the rack.
5. Run your fingers around the entire ring to check that there are no raised, twisted, or puckered sections.

<PIC>

When installed correctly, the sealing ring should fit snugly behind the sealing ring rack and should not fall out when the lid is turned over. If any part of the ring looks puckered or loose, remove it and reinstall it before cooking.

Important safety checks:
- Only install one sealing ring in the lid at a time.
- Use only authorized sealing rings made for this multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer.
- Do not use a sealing ring that is stretched, cut, deformed, or damaged.
- Before each pressure cooking use, check that the sealing ring is clean, properly seated, and in good condition.
- Replace the sealing ring every 12–18 months, or sooner if you notice stretching, deformation, cracks, or other damage.,['Manual30_16', 'Manual30_31']

To remove the silicone sealing ring from your multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer, please follow these steps:

1. **Turn the appliance off and unplug it.**  
   Make sure the cooker is fully unplugged and allow the lid and all parts to cool to room temperature before handling or cleaning.

2. **Remove the pressure cooking lid and turn it over.**  
   The silicone sealing ring is located on the underside of the pressure cooking lid, fitted behind the circular stainless-steel sealing ring rack.

3. **Pull out the sealing ring.**  
   Grip the edge of the silicone sealing ring with your fingers, then gently pull it outward and away from behind the stainless-steel sealing ring rack. Continue pulling around the lid until the entire ring comes free.

<PIC>

4. **Check the sealing ring rack after removal.**  
   Once the sealing ring is removed, inspect the stainless-steel rack. It should be securely attached, centered, and the same height all the way around the lid. If the rack looks bent, loose, uneven, or deformed, do not try to repair it yourself.

**Tip:** The sealing ring is usually easier to remove and reinstall when it is wet and soapy.

**Important safety reminder:** Only use an authorized sealing ring made for this multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer. Do not use a sealing ring that is stretched, cut, cracked, damaged, or deformed. Before cooking, always make sure the sealing ring is clean, properly seated, and in good condition.,['Manual30_30']

The sealing ring is the silicone ring fitted around the circular sealing ring rack on the underside of the pressure cooking lid. It is used with the pressure cooking lid, not the air fryer lid.

<PIC>

When the pressure cooking lid is closed, the sealing ring creates an airtight seal between the lid and the inner pot. This seal is essential for the cooker to build and maintain pressure safely. The sealing ring must be installed before pressure cooking, and only one sealing ring should be installed in the lid at a time.

<PIC>

A helpful tip: silicone can absorb strong aromas and certain flavours. If you often cook both savoury and sweet dishes, or foods with strong smells, keeping extra sealing rings can help reduce flavour transfer between meals.

Important safety notes:
- Only use authorized sealing rings designed for this multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer.
- Do not use a sealing ring that is stretched, cracked, cut, deformed, or damaged.
- Before cooking, always check that the sealing ring is clean, correctly installed, and sitting evenly in place.
- Sealing rings naturally stretch over time with regular use. Replace the sealing ring every 12–18 months, or sooner if you notice stretching, deformation, damage, or steam leaking from the sides of the lid.

If a damaged or incorrectly installed sealing ring is used, food or steam may escape from the cooker, which could cause injury or property damage.

To remove the sealing ring:
Grip the edge of the silicone ring and pull it out from behind the circular stainless-steel sealing ring rack. After removing it, check the steel rack to make sure it is secure, centered, and even all the way around the lid. If the rack is bent or deformed, do not try to repair it yourself.

<PIC>

To install the sealing ring:
Place the sealing ring over the sealing ring rack and press it firmly into place all the way around. Make sure it sits smoothly and does not pucker or bunch up. When properly installed, the sealing ring should fit snugly behind the rack and should not fall out when the lid is turned over.

<PIC>

If steam escapes from the sides of the lid during cooking, turn the appliance off and allow it to depressurize safely before opening. Then check that the sealing ring is clean, undamaged, and correctly installed.,['Manual30_4', 'Manual30_16', 'Manual30_30', 'Manual30_31']

The steam release valve is a key safety and pressure-control part on your multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer. It is located on the top of the pressure cooking lid, near the float valve and the quick release button.

<PIC>

The quick release button controls the steam release valve. In simple terms, this button determines whether the cooker stays sealed to build/hold pressure, or vents steam to release pressure.

<PIC>

The steam release valve sits loosely on the steam release pipe. This is normal—the valve is not meant to be fixed tightly in place.
