When pressure is released, hot steam comes out through the top of the steam release valve. Because it is part of the pressure-control system, it must be installed before pressure cooking.

<PIC>

For Quick Release, press the quick release button down until it clicks and locks into the Vent position. Once pressed, a strong jet of steam will release from the top of the steam release valve; this is expected. When all pressure has been released, the float valve will drop back down into the lid, indicating that the lid can be opened safely.

<PIC>

Important safety reminders:
- Do not cover, block, or obstruct the steam release valve in any way.
- Keep hands, face, and exposed skin away from the steam release valve when releasing pressure.
- Steam released from the valve is very hot and may cause burns.
- Do not try to remove or force open the lid while the float valve is raised. Wait until the float valve drops.
- Before pressure cooking, check that the steam release valve, steam release pipe, anti-block shield, and float valve are clean and not clogged.

For care and cleaning, you can remove the steam release valve by pulling it straight up and off the steam release pipe. Make sure the cooker is cool before handling parts.

<PIC>

To reinstall it, place the steam release valve back onto the steam release pipe and press down firmly. When installed correctly, it will still feel loose, but it should stay in place when the lid is turned over.

<PIC>,['Manual30_3', 'Manual30_14', 'Manual30_15', 'multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_01', 'Manual30_32', 'Manual30_33']

The quick release button is a pressure-control feature on the pressure cooking lid. It is located on the top of the lid, near the steam release valve and the float valve. You’ll use it when you need to seal the cooker for pressure cooking or release pressure after cooking.

<PIC>

The quick release button controls the steam release valve, which determines whether pressure stays inside the cooker or is released. In simple terms:

- Seal position: pressure is retained inside the cooker.
- Vent position: steam is released through the steam release valve.

<PIC>

What’s helpful about this design is that it’s easy to operate and helps keep your hands away from the hot steam during release.

A key point to know: although the lid may indicate “Turn to Seal,” the movement is more like a quick flick rather than a full turn. Do not rotate the quick release button more than 1/4 turn, or about 45°. After flicking it, allow it to spring back to its original position.

For Natural Release, leave the quick release button in the popped-up Seal position after cooking is complete, and turn Keep Warm off if you do not want additional heat applied. The cooker will depressurize gradually as the temperature drops. This can take different amounts of time depending on the food and liquid inside, and may take up to about 40 minutes. Once all pressure has been released, the float valve will drop down into the lid.

<PIC>

For Quick Release, press the quick release button down until it clicks and locks into the Vent position. This stops cooking more quickly and is useful for foods that can overcook easily, such as quick-cooking vegetables or delicate seafood.

<PIC>

When the button is pressed down into Vent, a strong jet of hot steam will eject from the top of the steam release valve. This is normal. Wait until the steam stops and the float valve drops into the lid before opening.

<PIC>

Important safety reminders:

- Do not place your hands, face, or exposed skin over the steam release valve when releasing pressure.
- Do not cover or block the steam release valve.
- Do not try to remove the lid while the float valve is still up.
- Never force the lid open; the float valve must be down before opening.
- Avoid using Quick Release for fatty, oily, thick, high-starch, or expanding foods such as stews, chili, pasta, congee, beans, or grains, as contents may splatter or foam during release.,['Manual30_3', 'Manual30_14', 'Manual30_9', 'multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_01', 'multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_02']

Natural Release (NR or NPR) is a pressure release method used after pressure cooking. Instead of venting steam immediately, the cooker cools down on its own, and the internal pressure gradually decreases over time.

<PIC>

Here’s what you need to know:

**How it works**
After the cooking program finishes, the heat reduces and cooking stops gradually. As the temperature inside the cooker drops, the pressure naturally releases without pressing the quick release button. During this time, the food may continue to cook slightly due to the remaining heat—this is called **carryover cooking** or **resting**.

**What to do**
- Leave the quick release button in the popped-up **Seal** position after cooking is complete.
- Turn **Keep Warm** off if you do not want extra heat to continue affecting the food.

<PIC>

**What to expect**
- Depressurization time varies depending on the amount and type of food inside.
- Natural Release can take up to about **40 minutes**.
- When all pressure has been released, the **float valve drops down into the lid**. This indicates the cooker is no longer pressurized and the lid can be opened safely.

<PIC>

**When to use Natural Release**
Natural Release is especially recommended for foods that may foam, splatter, or expand during cooking, such as:
- Soups, stews, chilis
- Pasta, oatmeal, congee
- Beans and grains
- Other high-starch or expanding foods

**Important safety reminder**
Do not try to remove the lid while the float valve is still up, and never force the lid open. The float valve must be fully down before opening the cooker.,['Manual30_9', 'Manual30_14', 'Manual30_18']

The robot anatomy of the vacuum is organized into three main areas: the top components, the control buttons and status indicators, and the bottom cleaning/charging components.

### 1. Top View
The top of the robot includes the main parts you’ll interact with when carrying the vacuum or removing the dust bin.

<PIC>

Key components:
- **iAdapt® Localization Camera**: Helps the robot recognize and navigate its environment.
- **Handle**: Used to lift or carry the robot when needed.
- **Bin Release Button**: Press this button to remove the dust bin for emptying or cleaning.

### 2. Buttons & Indicators
The control panel contains the main cleaning buttons and several status lights that show the robot’s current condition.

<PIC>

Buttons:
- **DOCK Button**: Sends the robot back to its charging base.
- **CLEAN Button**: Starts, pauses, or resumes a cleaning cycle.
- **SPOT Button**: Starts spot cleaning for a concentrated area of debris.

Indicators:
- **Wi-Fi® Indicator**: Shows Wi-Fi connection status.
- **Troubleshooting Indicator**: Lights up when the robot needs attention or has encountered an issue.
- **Battery Indicator**: Shows battery/charging status.
- **Full Bin Indicator**: Alerts you when the dust bin is full or needs attention.
- **Dirt Detect™ Indicator**: Lights when the robot detects a dirtier area and cleans it more thoroughly.

### 3. Bottom View
The underside contains the parts responsible for cleaning, sensing the floor, and charging.

<PIC>

Key components:
- **Debris Extractors**: The main rollers that lift dirt, hair, and debris from the floor.
- **Extractor Caps**: End caps on the extractors that can be removed for cleaning hair or debris buildup.
- **Extractor Frame**: Holds the debris extractors in place.
- **Extractor Frame Release Tabs**: Tabs used to open the extractor frame for maintenance.
- **Dust Bin**: Collects the debris picked up during cleaning.
- **Side Brush**: Sweeps dirt from edges and corners into the robot’s cleaning path.
- **Charging Contacts**: Connect with the charging base to recharge the robot.
- **Floor Tracking Sensor**: Helps the robot track movement and navigate across the floor.,['Manual32_1', 'Manual32_2', 'Manual32_3']

The two primary modes are Halo Mode and Virtual Wall Mode. They are set using the Dual Mode Virtual Wall Barrier, which helps control where the vacuum can and cannot clean based on your home’s layout and cleaning needs. 1. Halo Mode Use Halo Mode when you want to protect a specific object or small area. Move the switch to the down position, and the device creates an invisible circular protective zone that the vacuum will not enter. This is especially useful for keeping the vacuum away from items such as: - Pet bowls - Vases - Delicate objects - Corners or spaces under desks that you do not want the vacuum to enter The protected “halo” area reaches approximately 24 inches from the center of the device, creating about a 4-foot / 1.2-meter protective zone around the item. 2. Virtual Wall Mode Use Virtual Wall Mode when you want to block the vacuum from entering a room, hallway, doorway, or other opening. Move the switch to the up position, and the device creates an invisible, cone-shaped barrier that only the vacuum can detect. This mode can block openings of up to 10 feet / 3 meters. The barrier becomes wider as it extends farther from the device, making it helpful for separating cleaning areas or preventing the vacuum from crossing into places you do not want cleaned. Battery note: Under normal use, the Virtual Wall Barrier batteries typically last 8–10 months. If you will not be using the device for an extended period, switch it to the Off middle position to help preserve battery life.,['Manual32_4', 'Manual32_5', 'Manual32_6']

To clean the front caster wheel properly, please follow these steps:

1. Turn the vacuum over and locate the front caster wheel. Pull firmly upward on the front wheel to remove the wheel assembly from its housing.

4. Clear away any hair or debris wrapped around the axle or trapped inside the wheel.

The dual-mode virtual wall barrier is used to control where the vacuum can and cannot go during cleaning. It helps keep the vacuum in the areas you want cleaned and prevents it from entering areas you want to protect or avoid. You can choose one of two modes depending on your home layout and cleaning needs.

<PIC>

**1. Halo Mode — protects objects or small areas**  
When the switch is moved to the **down** position, the device enters **Halo Mode**. In this mode, it creates an invisible protective zone around itself that the vacuum will not enter.

This is useful for protecting items such as pet bowls, vases, plant stands, or any object you do not want the vacuum to bump into. It can also help keep the vacuum away from tight corners or spaces under furniture. The protective halo reaches approximately **24 inches from the center of the device**.

<PIC>

**2. Virtual Wall Mode — blocks doorways or openings**  
When the switch is moved to the **up** position, the device works as a **Virtual Wall**. It creates an invisible cone-shaped barrier that only the vacuum can detect, stopping the vacuum from crossing into another area.

This mode is best for blocking doorways, hallway entrances, or room openings. It can block openings up to approximately **10 feet**. Please note that the barrier becomes wider the farther it extends from the device.

<PIC>

**Battery and storage tip:** Under normal use, the batteries usually last about **8–10 months**. If you will not be using the virtual wall barrier for an extended period, switch it to the **middle “Off” position** before storing it.,['Manual32_4', 'Manual32_5', 'Manual32_6']

If the vacuum detects a problem, it will alert you in three ways: it will play a two-tone distress sound, then announce a spoken message describing the issue, and the troubleshooting indicator will blink. You can identify the troubleshooting indicator on the control panel as shown below:



For some issues, rebooting the vacuum may clear the error. Please try the following steps:

1. Press and hold the CLEAN button for about 10 seconds, until all indicators light up.
2. Release the CLEAN button.
3. After releasing it, you should hear a tone confirming that the reboot was successful.
4. Once rebooted, check whether the troubleshooting indicator stops blinking and whether the vacuum can operate normally again.

If the issue continues after rebooting, please use the app to check the vacuum’s current status and follow the specific guidance shown there. Additional support and troubleshooting videos are also available through the app and online.

Note: If you use the scheduling feature, open the app after rebooting to confirm that your cleaning schedule is still saved correctly.,['Manual32_2']

To mount the camera or compatible equipment on a drop-ceiling T-rail, use the included T-rail mounting hardware. The hardware is designed to fit most common T-rail sizes, including 9/16", 15/16", and 1 1/2" rails.

Before starting, confirm that you have the T-rail clips, set screws, hex key, mount plate, and the correct screws for your T-rail type.

1. Set the correct spacing for the T-rail clips
Use the dashed guide lines on the mount plate template to determine the correct spacing between the two T-rail clips. Align the clips so they match the marked T-rail mounting positions on the template. This ensures the clips will line up correctly with the mount plate holes later.

<PIC>

2. Secure the T-rail clips with the set screws
After positioning the clips, tighten the set screws on each T-rail clip. Use the included 5/64" (2 mm) hex key to secure them firmly. Make sure both clips are tightened evenly so the mount remains stable once installed.

<PIC>

3. Attach the mount plate to the T-rail clips
Position the mount plate under the T-rail clips and fasten it using the mount plate holes marked with “G”. These “G” holes are the designated holes for drop-ceiling T-rail mounting. Insert the screws through the mount plate and into the clips, then tighten until the plate is secure.

<PIC>

4. Snap the T-rail clips onto the ceiling T-rail
Attach the T-rail clips to the ceiling rail by rotating them into place until they snap securely onto the T-rail. After installation, the black foam pads on the clips should be slightly compressed. This compression helps keep the mounting assembly snug and stable against the rail.

<PIC>

5. Select the correct screws based on the T-rail type
For standard T-rail installations, use the standard 6-32 x 4 mm T-rail screws.

For recessed T-rail installations, use the recessed mounting configuration: install the rubber spacers between the mount plate and the clips, and use the longer 6-32 x 7 mm screws. The spacers compensate for the recessed rail height and help keep the mount plate properly aligned.

<PIC>

Once the mount plate is firmly attached and the clips are locked onto the T-rail, you can proceed with attaching the camera or equipment to the mounted plate according to the required orientation and cabling path.,['Manual33_11', 'Manual33_12', 'Manual33_13', 'Manual33_10', 'Manual33_14']

The brake lever is used to slow or stop the machine by braking the entire drive system. To stop, first release the throttle, then squeeze the brake lever smoothly toward the handlebar. When the brake is applied, the brake light will illuminate.

<PIC>

Please also check the brake lever position: the end of the brake lever should not extend beyond the end of the handlebar. Keeping it within the handlebar end helps prevent the lever from being damaged if the machine is placed on its side for service.

<PIC>

For the parking brake button, use it when parking the machine or before starting the engine:
1. Squeeze and hold the brake lever.
2. Push down the parking brake button.
3. While holding the button down, slowly release the brake lever so the parking brake locks in place.

To release the parking brake, simply squeeze the brake lever again; the parking brake will disengage.

<PIC>

Important safety notes:
- Always set the parking brake before attempting to start the engine.
- Do not operate or ride the machine with the parking brake applied, as this can overheat the brake disc and reduce braking performance.
- When slowing down or stopping, apply the brake gently rather than suddenly. Sudden or improper braking can cause the drive track to lose traction, reducing control and increasing the risk of an accident.
- On icy or hard-packed snow, stopping distances can be much longer, so slow down early and plan ahead.,['Manual34_39', 'Manual34_40', 'Manual34_133']

Before using a snowmobile, take a few minutes to complete the following pre-operation checks. These checks help confirm that the machine is ready to start, safe to control, and properly equipped for the ride.

<PIC>

1. Check the safety labels and controls
Review all warning and instruction labels on the snowmobile, and make sure you understand the location and function of the operating controls before starting. Pay particular attention to the throttle, brake, steering, engine stop switch, parking brake, and any drive/reverse selector positions if equipped.

2. Check the fuel level and fuel cap
Make sure there is enough fuel in the tank for your ride. After refueling, confirm that the fuel tank cap is closed securely. Keep snow or ice out of the tank while refueling, and wipe up any spilled fuel immediately.

<PIC>

3. Check the engine oil level
Confirm that the oil tank has sufficient oil before starting the engine. Use the recommended oil: YAMALUBE 2-cycle oil. Do not operate the snowmobile if the oil level is too low.

<PIC>

4. Check throttle lever operation
Before every start, press and release the throttle lever to make sure it moves smoothly and returns fully to the idle position when released. If the lever sticks, feels rough, or does not return properly, do not start or operate the snowmobile until it is corrected.

<PIC>

5. Check the manual starter
Inspect the starter rope and handle before use. Make sure the rope is not frayed, damaged, or likely to break, and confirm that the starter can be pulled normally.

<PIC>

6. Check the brake, parking brake, and steering
Check that the brake operates properly and that the steering moves normally before starting the engine. Apply the parking brake before starting, but never ride with the parking brake applied, as this can overheat the brake and reduce braking ability.

<PIC>

7. Inspect major running parts and equipment
Before riding, also check the drive track, slide runners, ski and ski runners, lights, battery, fittings and fasteners. Make sure the drive belt and belt guard are properly in place, and carry basic service tools and spare parts such as a tool kit, flashlight, tape, tow rope, emergency starter rope, spare drive belt, light bulbs, and spark plugs when appropriate.

<PIC>

8. Wear proper protective clothing
Wear an approved helmet, face shield or goggles, a suitable snowmobile suit, boots, and snowmobile gloves or mittens that still allow you to operate the controls safely.

<PIC>

Important safety reminders:
- Do not operate the snowmobile after drinking alcohol or taking drugs.
- Do not allow anyone to stand behind the machine while starting, inspecting, or adjusting it.
- Handle fuel carefully: never refuel while smoking, near an open flame, or while the engine is running or hot.
- Do not operate on public roads.
- This snowmobile is intended for the operator only; do not carry passengers.
- Avoid running the engine in an enclosed area because exhaust fumes are dangerous.,['Manual34_7', 'Manual34_93', 'Manual34_96', 'Manual34_97', 'Manual34_98', 'Manual34_108', 'Manual34_107', 'Manual34_11']

Before starting the snowmobile engine, please make sure the machine is in a safe condition: apply the parking brake, confirm the throttle/brake/steering operate normally, ensure no one is standing behind the snowmobile, and check that the engine stop switch is in the “ON/RUN” position.

1. Fully open the starter lever/choke for a cold engine start. This enriches the fuel mixture and helps the engine start more easily.
<PIC>

2. Pull the engine stop switch up to the “RUN” position. If this switch is pushed down, the engine will not start.
<PIC>

3. For a manual starting model: turn the main switch to the “ON” position. Then pull the recoil starter slowly until you feel it engage, and pull it briskly to start the engine.
<PIC>
<PIC>

4. For an electric starting model: turn the main switch to the “START” position to crank the engine.
<PIC>

5. Once the engine starts, move the starter lever/choke to the “Half-open” position. Let the engine warm up until it runs smoothly and does not run roughly or begin to stall when the starter lever is moved to the “Close” position.

Important for electric starting: release the switch immediately after the engine starts. If the engine does not start, release the switch, wait a few seconds, and try again. Keep each starting attempt as short as possible to protect the battery, and do not crank the engine for more than 10 seconds at a time.,['Manual34_110', 'Manual34_111', 'Manual34_115', 'Manual34_116', 'Manual34_117']

**Important:** Adjust the engine idle speed first before adjusting the throttle cable. This helps ensure the throttle free play is set correctly.

1. **Locate the throttle cable adjuster and locknut.**
   - The **locknut** is marked as **②**.
   - The **adjuster** is marked as **③**.

2. **Loosen the adjuster locknut ②.**

3. **Turn the adjuster ③ in or out** until the throttle lever free play is within the correct specification.

<PIC>

**Throttle lever free play ①:**
- **BR250T / ET410TR / VK540E:** 1.0–2.0 mm (0.04–0.08 in)
- **CS340E:** 0.5–1.0 mm (0.02–0.04 in)

Use the following diagrams to identify the measurement point for the free play and the adjustment parts:

<PIC>

<PIC>

4. **Tighten the locknut ②** after the correct free play is achieved.

5. **Check throttle operation.**
   After adjustment, make sure the throttle lever moves smoothly and returns properly when released. If the throttle does not move freely or does not return correctly, do not ride the snowmobile until it is checked and corrected.,['Manual34_148', 'Manual34_149', 'Manual34_150']

Before riding, make sure the steering system is working correctly and that you understand how to use it safely.

1. Pre-ride steering check
Before starting the engine or operating the snowmobile, check the throttle, brake, and steering for proper operation. For the steering system, move the handlebars and the skis side to side to check for excessive play, and also check the steering column for any looseness. If the handlebars, skis, or steering column feel loose or abnormal, do not ride until the issue has been corrected.

<PIC>

2. Know the steering components
The steering handlebar controls the steering ski. Make sure you can clearly identify the handlebar, the steering ski, and the related front steering area before riding, so you know how the machine will respond when you turn.

<PIC>

3. Proper steering and turning technique
When riding, always keep both hands on the handlebars and keep your feet on the running boards. Never place your feet outside the running boards while the snowmobile is moving.

To make a turn correctly:
- Slow down before entering the curve.
- Turn the handlebars in the direction you want to go.
- Shift your weight onto the running board on the inside of the turn.
- Lean your upper body into the turn to help keep the snowmobile balanced.

<PIC>

Practice this technique many times at low speed in a large, flat, open area with no obstacles or traffic. As your speed increases or the turn becomes sharper, you may need to lean more into the turn. Avoid higher speeds until you are fully familiar with the snowmobile’s handling.

4. Extra caution on ice
On ice or icy surfaces, steering control is greatly reduced. Ride slowly and carefully, and avoid rapid acceleration, sudden turning, or hard braking, as these can cause loss of control or an uncontrolled spin.,['Manual34_108', 'Manual34_13', 'Manual34_127']

To turn a snowmobile correctly on most snow surfaces, the key is using your body weight—often called “body English”—together with smooth steering and speed control.

1. **Slow down before the curve**  
   As you approach the turn, reduce your speed early. Avoid sudden braking or abrupt throttle changes, as they can reduce traction and make the snowmobile unstable.

2. **Begin steering in the direction you want to go**  
   Turn the handlebars smoothly toward the direction of the curve. Keep both hands on the handlebars and both feet on the running boards.

3. **Shift your weight to the inside of the turn**  
   Put more weight on the running board on the inside of the turn. At the same time, lean your upper body into the turn. This helps the snowmobile stay balanced and improves control.

<PIC>

4. **Adjust your lean based on speed and turn sharpness**  
   The sharper the turn or the higher the speed, the more you will need to lean into the turn. Use smooth, controlled movements rather than sudden body shifts.

5. **Practice at low speed first**  
   Practice this technique many times in a large, flat, open area with no obstacles or traffic. Once you are comfortable, you can gradually try tighter curves or slightly higher speeds.

6. **If the snowmobile starts to tip**  
   Lean farther into the turn to help regain balance. If needed, gradually release the throttle and/or steer slightly toward the outside of the turn. Do not make sudden corrections.

**Important safety reminder:** Avoid higher speeds until you are fully familiar with your snowmobile’s handling, controls, and turning behavior.,['Manual34_127']

When riding uphill on a snowmobile, use a controlled approach and keep your body weight positioned to maintain traction and balance.

<PIC>

1. Start with gentle slopes first. Practice on mild hills until you are comfortable with throttle control, balance, and braking. Only attempt steeper or more difficult climbs after you have developed enough riding skill.

2. Build speed before the climb. As you approach the hill, accelerate before you start going up. Once the climb begins, reduce the throttle opening slightly to help prevent the track from spinning or slipping.

3. Keep your weight on the uphill side. Proper body position is very important. When climbing straight up a hill, lean forward to keep the front of the snowmobile stable and improve control. On steeper inclines, stand on the running boards and lean forward over the handlebars.

4. Slow down near the top. As you reach the crest of the hill, reduce speed and be ready for unexpected hazards, such as obstacles, sharp drop-offs, other riders, vehicles, or people on the other side.

5. If you cannot continue uphill, do not spin the track. Stop the engine and set the parking brake. Then pull the rear of the snowmobile around so the machine points back down the hill. Stay on the uphill side of the snowmobile—do not stand or move to the downhill side. Once the snowmobile is facing downhill, restart the engine, release the parking brake, and descend carefully.

Warning: Side hills and steep slopes are not recommended for beginner or novice snowmobile riders.,['Manual34_127']

When riding downhill on a snowmobile, the key is to stay slow, controlled, and avoid letting the machine coast freely down the slope.

1. Keep your speed as low as practical before and during the descent. Do not start downhill too fast, as it becomes much harder to regain control once momentum builds.

2. Apply just enough throttle to keep the clutch engaged while going down the hill. This is important because it allows engine compression to help slow the snowmobile, instead of allowing the machine to roll freely downhill.

3. Use the brake frequently, but only with light pressure. Gentle, repeated braking helps control speed without upsetting the track’s grip.

<PIC>

4. Keep your body balanced and stay alert to the slope conditions. Snow, ice, and hard-packed surfaces can greatly reduce traction, so plan your descent early and avoid sudden movements.

WARNING: Use extra caution when braking during a downhill descent. Excessive braking can cause the track to lock, which may lead to loss of control.,['Manual34_128']

Crossing the face of a slope—also called side hilling—requires good balance, careful speed control, and the correct body position. This technique is not recommended for beginner or novice snowmobile riders.

When traveling across a slope:

1. Keep your weight on the uphill side. Lean your upper body toward the uphill side of the slope to help counteract the snowmobile’s tendency to roll or slide downhill.
2. Use the recommended side-hill riding position: place the knee of your downhill leg on the seat, and keep the foot of your uphill leg on the uphill running board.
3. Stay flexible and ready to shift your weight. This position makes it easier to move your body quickly if the snowmobile starts to slide, lean, or tip.

<PIC>

Because snow and ice can be slippery, be prepared for the snowmobile to slide sideways while crossing the slope. If it begins to slip sideways and there are no obstacles in your path, steer in the direction of the slide. Once you regain balance and control, gradually steer back toward the direction you want to travel.

If the snowmobile starts to tip, steer down the hill to help regain balance.

Warning: If you cannot maintain proper balance and the snowmobile is going to tip over, get off immediately on the uphill side. This helps keep you away from the machine if it rolls or slides downhill.,['Manual34_129']

To inspect the spark plug on your snowmobile, follow these steps:

1. **Remove and inspect the spark plug periodically.**  
   The spark plug is an important engine component and its condition can give clues about overall engine condition. Over time, heat and deposits can cause the plug to break down or erode, so regular inspection is recommended.

2. **Check the color of the porcelain insulator.**  
   Look at the white porcelain area around the center electrode. For normal riding conditions, the ideal color is **medium to light tan**.  
   - If the color is very different, there may be an engine-related issue.  
   - For example, a very white porcelain color may indicate an air leak or carburetion issue.  
   If you notice abnormal coloring, it is best to have the snowmobile checked by your dealer rather than trying to diagnose it yourself.

3. **Confirm the spark plug has the correct thread length, also called “reach.”**  
   Using the wrong reach can cause engine problems:  
   - If the reach is **too long**, overheating and engine damage may occur.  
   - If the reach is **too short**, plug fouling, poor performance, carbon buildup, hot spots, and thread damage may result.  
   Always use a spark plug with the proper reach for your model.

   <PIC>

4. **Measure and adjust the electrode gap before installation.**  
   Before installing any spark plug, use a **wire thickness gauge** to measure the electrode gap. Adjust the gap to the specified value if needed. In the diagram below, item **1** shows the spark plug reach, and item **2** shows the electrode gap.

   <PIC>

5. **Clean the plug and mounting area before reinstalling.**  
   Before installing the spark plug:  
   - Clean the gasket seating surface.  
   - Wipe any dirt or grime from the threads.  
   - Install the plug carefully to avoid cross-threading.

6. **Tighten the spark plug to the correct torque.**  
   Spark plug tightening torque: **28 Nm** *(2.8 m·kg, 20 ft·lb)*.

**Note:** Before switching to a different type of spark plug, consult your dealer to ensure it is suitable for your snowmobile.,['Manual34_138', 'Manual34_139']

To clean a snowmobile properly, follow these steps:

1. Clean the entire machine thoroughly, both inside and outside, to remove corrosive salts, acids, dirt, and other buildup.
2. Apply Mud and Grease Release, or an equivalent cleaner, to loosen mud, grease, and grime from affected areas.
3. Wash the snowmobile using mild soap and water. Avoid harsh cleaners that may damage finishes or components.
4. Rinse the machine thoroughly to remove all soap and loosened residue.
5. Dry the snowmobile completely, paying attention to areas where moisture can collect, to help prevent corrosion.

Important: If the snowmobile was transported or used in areas where road salt is present, clean it as soon as you arrive at your destination. Road salt can be highly corrosive, so removing it promptly helps protect metal parts and maintain the machine’s condition.

Poor TV or radio reception is usually caused by signal interference, weak signal strength, antenna issues, or temporary station-related problems. The most common causes and conditions include the following:

1. Ignition or electrical interference
This type of interference can make the picture flutter or drift, and may cause black spots, horizontal streaks, buzzing, or unstable sound. It is often caused by nearby automobile ignition systems, neon lamps, electric drills, or other household electrical appliances operating close to the TV, radio, antenna, or signal cable.

<PIC>

2. Ghosting or multiple images
Ghosting happens when the broadcast signal reaches the antenna through more than one path. One signal arrives directly, while another is reflected from tall buildings, hills, towers, or other large objects. This can create a faint duplicate image or echo-like reception. Adjusting the antenna direction, height, or position may improve the signal.

<PIC>

3. Snow or speckled picture
A snowy picture usually means the receiver is in a weak-signal or fringe reception area. The image may be covered with small dots, and the sound may also become noisy. In this situation, improving the antenna connection, using a better-positioned outdoor antenna, or installing a more suitable antenna may help strengthen reception.

<PIC>

Other common causes can include:
- Strong random interference from nearby cars, airplanes, or other moving sources.
- A weak antenna signal.
- Loose, damaged, or poorly connected antenna/cable wiring.
- Local interference from nearby electrical devices or metal objects.
- A temporary issue with the broadcast station.
- The antenna direction or position needing adjustment.
- The channel not being stored correctly or needing to be programmed again.

Recommended checks:
- Try another channel to confirm whether the issue is limited to one station.
- Check that the antenna or cable connection is secure.
- Move possible interference sources away from the TV/radio and antenna cable.
- Adjust the antenna direction or position.
- Adjust the picture settings if the picture is unstable.
- Run AUTO PROGRAM again to search and store available channels, especially if the received signal quality is poor.

<PIC>,['television0_01', 'television0_02', 'television0_03', 'Manual35_45']

To enable and customize captions and on-screen text settings on the TV, you can use either the dedicated CAPTION button for quick switching or the MENU button for a more guided setup.

Closed captions display the dialogue and narration of TV programs as on-screen text, similar to subtitles. They are especially useful when you want to read the program audio or when listening conditions are difficult.

<PIC>

### Option 1: Use the CAPTION button for quick setup
1. Press the **CAPTION** button on the remote control.
2. Press the **CAPTION** button repeatedly to cycle through the available options:
   - **OFF**: Turns captions/text off.
   - **Mode1**: Usually displays the main caption signal.
   - **Mode2**: May display a second caption signal, such as demonstration or program information.
   - **Text1 / Text2**: Displays full-screen text services when provided by the broadcaster.
3. When the desired option is shown, press the **ENTER (O)** button to exit.

<PIC>

### Option 2: Use the MENU button
1. Press the **MENU** button.
2. Use the **up/down** buttons to select the **Special** menu.
3. Press the **right** button, then use the **up/down** buttons to select **Caption**.
4. Press the **right** button again, then use the **up/down** buttons to choose one of the following options: **Off**, **Mode1**, **Mode2**, **Text1**, or **Text2**.
5. Press the **MENU** button repeatedly to exit the menu.

<PIC>

### Additional notes
- **Mode1** is usually the best choice for standard closed captions.
- **Mode2** may be used by some stations for alternate caption information, demonstrations, or program-related details.
- **Text1/Text2** are full-screen text services, but not all stations provide them.
- The TV remembers the last caption mode you selected, even after the TV is turned off.
- Not every broadcast includes closed caption signals. If captions do not appear, the program may not support captions.
- Poor reception, weak antenna signal, interference, or low-quality recorded content may affect caption or text display. In text mode, a blank black box may appear and disappear if the signal is poor; this is normal under weak-signal conditions.,['Manual35_36', 'Manual35_37', 'Manual35_38']

For optimal signal reception, we recommend using an outdoor antenna and making sure both the antenna and its cable are in good condition. Severely weathered antennas or damaged antenna cables can reduce signal quality, so please inspect them before connecting.

1. Connect the outdoor antenna to the TV

1. Attach the 300 ohm flat wire to the screws on the 300 ohm to 75 ohm adapter.
2. Insert the end of the 300 ohm to 75 ohm adapter into the TV’s 75 ohm antenna jack.

For a 75 Ohm coaxial cable:
1. Connect the 75 ohm coaxial cable directly to the TV’s 75 ohm antenna jack.

<PIC>

2. Turn on the TV and run AUTO PROGRAM

After the antenna is connected, turn on the TV and use AUTO PROGRAM so the TV can search for and memorize all active channels available in your area.

Using the MENU button:

2. Press the ▲/▼ buttons until the CH. menu is displayed.
3. Press ▶, then use ▲/▼ to select Auto prog.

The screen below shows the Auto prog. progress display and the Stop option.

Additional tips for better reception:
· If the memorized channels have poor picture or sound quality, run Auto prog. again.
· AUTO PROGRAM stores all receivable channels, including RF or cable signals if connected.
· If you see ghosts, snow, lines, or weak signal, adjust the direction or position of the outdoor antenna and then run AUTO PROGRAM again.

For the best picture and sound, connect the DVD player using the TV’s COMPONENT DVD INPUT (480i) terminals and the matching left/right audio inputs.

1. Turn off the TV and DVD player before connecting cables.

2. Connect the component video cables from the DVD player to the TV:
- DVD Y output → TV Y input
- DVD Pb/PB output → TV PB input
- DVD Pr/PR output → TV PR input

Then connect the audio cables:
- DVD L audio output → TV L audio input
- DVD R audio output → TV R audio input

<PIC>

3. Check the labels carefully, because different DVD players may use slightly different names for the same component video terminals. Match them as follows:
- Y → Y
- Cb / B-Y / Pb / PB → PB
- Cr / R-Y / Pr / PR → PR

<PIC>

4. If you want to play sound through an audio system instead of the TV speakers, connect the DVD player’s L/R audio outputs to the audio system’s L/R audio input, then select the correct input source on that audio system.

5. Turn on the DVD player first, then turn on the TV. Press the TV/VIDEO button on the remote control or on the TV front panel until COMPONENT is selected.

<PIC>

Note: The TV remembers the last selected input mode even after it is turned off, so if it was last set to COMPONENT, it may return to that mode the next time you power it on.,['Manual35_43', 'Manual35_44', 'Manual35_0']

During depressurization, please follow these safety precautions to ensure safe operation:

<PIC>

Even after cooking has finished, the cooker can still be very hot and pressurized. Before opening the lid, the pressure must be fully released and the float valve must be completely down.

**1. Never force the lid open**  
Do **not** attempt to remove the lid while the float valve is up. Never force the lid open under any circumstances. The contents may still be under extreme pressure, and opening the lid too early can cause hot food or steam to escape suddenly, which may result in serious injury or property damage.

**2. Keep hands, face, and skin away from the steam release valve**  
Steam released from the steam release valve is extremely hot. When releasing pressure, do **not** place your hands, face, or any exposed skin over or near the valve. Also, do **not** cover the steam release valve, as this can interfere with proper venting.

**3. Use the correct pressure-release method for the food being cooked**  
Do **not** use Quick Release when cooking fatty, oily, thick, or high-starch foods, such as stews, chilis, pasta, or congee. Also avoid Quick Release for foods that expand during cooking, such as beans and grains, because they may foam, splatter, or clog the valve.

**4. Wait until it is safe before opening**  
Only open the lid after pressure has been fully released and the float valve has dropped. If the valve is still raised, the cooker is not safe to open.

**5. Follow the recommended venting method**  
Use the pressure-release method recommended for your recipe. This helps prevent splattering, clogging, or sudden steam release, and ensures the cooker can be opened safely.,['Manual30_8']

You can adjust the intensity on your electric toothbrush directly from the handle, or customize it in the app to match your oral care needs.

Your toothbrush has 3 intensity levels, shown by the vertical intensity indicator lights on the handle:

- High intensity: 3 lights — for a stronger clean
- Medium intensity: 2 lights — for everyday balanced cleaning
- Low intensity: 1 light — for a gentler brushing experience, such as sensitive gums or teeth

<PIC>

To change the intensity manually, press the intensity indicator area on the handle. Each press cycles through the available levels, so you can select the setting that feels most comfortable for you.

You can change the intensity:
- Before brushing
- During brushing
- After brushing

<PIC>

You can also adjust the intensity through the app, where additional toothbrush settings can be customized.

Please note: If Adaptive Intensity is enabled and you apply too much pressure for an extended period, the toothbrush may automatically reduce the intensity by 1 level to help protect your gums. At the start of each new brushing cycle, the toothbrush will return to your pre-selected intensity setting.,['toothbrush0_06', 'toothbrush0_07']

You can customize your toothbrush by activating or deactivating these features:

- Adaptive Intensity
- Pressure Sensor Feedback
- Scrubbing Feedback
- Brush head replacement reminder

Please note: If Pressure Sensor Feedback is deactivated, Adaptive Intensity will also be deactivated automatically.

There are two ways to adjust these settings:

1. Through the app
2. Directly from the toothbrush handle

App controls

From the app, you can activate or deactivate:

- Adaptive Intensity
- Scrubbing Feedback

Make sure your toothbrush is paired with the app and Bluetooth is enabled on your phone before changing these settings.

Handle controls

You can also activate or deactivate selected features directly from the handle by following the steps below.

Step 1: Place the toothbrush handle on the charging stand.

<PIC>

Step 2: Press and hold the power button on the handle.

<PIC>

Keep holding the power button until you reach the feature you want to change. The toothbrush will use beeps and indicator lights to show which feature is selected:

- Adaptive Intensity: hold for up to 3 seconds  
  You will hear 1 beep, and the SenseIQ indicator will light up for 3 seconds.

- Pressure Sensor Feedback: hold for up to 5 seconds  
  You will hear 1 beep, then 2 beeps. The SenseIQ indicator and the light ring will light up purple for 3 seconds.

- Brush head replacement reminder: hold for up to 7 seconds  
  You will hear 1 beep, then 2 beeps, then 3 beeps. The brush head replacement reminder indicator will light up for 3 seconds.

<PIC>

Confirmation after changing a setting

After you release the button, check the battery indicator and tones to confirm whether the feature was activated or deactivated:

- Activated: the battery indicator blinks white 3 times, and you hear 3 tones from low to high.
- Deactivated: the battery indicator blinks amber 3 times, and you hear 3 tones from high to low.

In short, place the handle on the charger, press and hold the power button for the required time, then use the light color and tone pattern to confirm whether the selected feature has been turned on or off.,['Manual37_16', 'Manual37_13', 'toothbrush0_11']
